The impression is that of focused activity, a mind deeply engaged in a complex task – consistent with someone immersed in archival research. There's also a faint undercurrent of success or discovery in her emotional state, a subtle hum of excitement beneath her usual composed focus.

A moment after Wes sends his comm, his bracer chirps with Marina's reply.

Marina's voice comes over the commlink voice. While hurried and curt it carries that spark of discovery Wes had sensed. It's clear her trip to the Archives hasn't been in vain.
**"Wes, it's Marina. Copy that on the 13:45 embarkation. I'm still in the Main Archives on Deck 6, but I think I've found something significant. Cross-referencing some very old Pathfinder logs with general schematics for pre-Republic void-barges. I'm downloading the relevant fragments now. Give me ten minutes to secure the data and get to Hangar C-7. I'll brief you and the team en route or as soon as I'm aboard. Cloudspinner out."**

As he waited for her response he reached out with the Force.  It was easier this time to get a sense of where she was or more where she wasn’t.  The combination of the two gave him a sense that she was still in the archives.  What he also gleaned from their connection was that she had found something.  Something worthwhile to be pushing the limits of the mission time frame.  Her excitement was palpable.   He looked at Echo and Sera giving them both a small nod as he flashed a quick smile.  **”She's found something in the archives.  It’s enough to cause her some excitement or satisfaction.”**  As if on queue his comms indicated she was responding and he let it play so the others could hear.  What he had felt was accurate and she reassured she would be there on time.  **”Copy that.  Engines are running, don't be late.  Del-Fin out.”**


Taking his helmet with both hands he reached up and put it into place.  **”Sera monitor comms chatter.  Let me know if anything comes up.  Roric, Echo check the gear in the cargo hold, make sure it’s all secured and feel free to check the ion gear.  I already looked it over but another set of eyes on it won’t hurt.  Everyone check in at 13:45.”**  With that he turned and moved towards the cockpit to check on Kael and the situation up there.

Sera and Echo exchange a quick, subtle glance as Wes relays Marina's status and then plays her comm message. There's perhaps a flicker of curiosity, maybe even slight surprise, in their expressions at Wes's almost preternatural certainty about Marina's emotional state *before* her message arrived. They're professionals, likely accustomed to Jedi intuition, but the depth of connection Wes seems to share with Padawan Cloudspinner is... noteworthy. Roric, ever stoic, simply nods, his focus already shifting to the practical task of checking the gear.

**"Understood, Wes,"** Sera replies, her voice professional but with a hint of intrigued respect. **"I'll monitor all local and ship-to-team channels. If Marina's found something juicy in those old archives, it could give us a real edge."** She moves towards one of the auxiliary terminals in the hold, likely to set up a dedicated monitoring station away from the cockpit's main comms.
**"Acknowledged,"** Roric grunts, already turning towards the aft storage compartment where the ion weapons are stowed. **"Echo, with me. We'll give everything a final once-over."**

Echo gives a sharp nod to Wes and then to Roric, falling into step behind him as they head towards the rear of the *Void Skimmer*. **"Will do. Redundancy in checks is never a bad thing, especially with unfamiliar ordnance."**

The three team members disperse to their assigned tasks, leaving Wes to head to the cockpit. The hold of the *Void Skimmer* is now filled with a quiet hum of focused activity as they make their final preparations. The ship's internal chronometer reads 13:30. Fifteen minutes until the designated final check-in.

As Wes ascends the ladder to the cockpit, he finds Kael already there, running through the final stages of the pre-flight checklist, his fingers dancing across the illuminated controls. The main viewports show the bustling activity of Hangar C-7, ground crews making their last checks on the other departing shuttles.

Kael glances up as Wes enters, his expression unchanged. **"Del-Fin. Systems are green across the board. Astrogation data integrated. She's ready to fly on your command. Hangar control just confirmed the other two teams are on similar timelines. We're looking at a synchronized launch at 1400 hours."** He taps a control. **"External ramp is still open for Cloudspinner. Any word on her ETA?"**

Wes nodded as Sera, Roric and Echo all set out to what was requested of them.  As he walked the short distance to the ladder to the cockpit he reflected on the feeling that he picked up from Marina.   She was excited at what she had found.  It must have been promising for her to be cutting the time frame as short as she was.  She wasn’t new at this and he trusted her.  She said she’d be there in time.  


Stepping off the ladder Wes could see that Kael was expertly tending to the preflight checklist as his hands deftly moved over the controls.  Looking out the viewports the importance of the three missions hit home once again.  With all three successful it would mean critical supplies for the *Chu’unthor* and several more cycles at least until they need more supplies.   Kael’s report was short and to the point.  All systems were green.  Astrogation was read and the tight time frame was still on point.   All that was needed was Marina.  Looking at the synced chrono that was ticking by on his HUD he saw the time 13:32.  Still thirteen minutes for the final check in.   


**”She’ll be here.  She found some solid intel in the archives.  She was finishing retrieving it and then getting here.  From what she had said she had found significant data but she will cut it close for the 13:45 check in.”**

Kael grunts, accepting Wes's assessment of Marina's timing without comment. He continues to monitor the *Void Skimmer's* systems, his gaze flicking between various readouts. **"Archives can be a black hole for time,"** he remarks, a rare hint of something other than pure pragmatism in his tone. **"Just so long as her 'solid intel' doesn't include any ancient curses or self-destruct sequences for ships like this one."** He taps a sensor display. **"External pressure is nominal. Hangar atmosphere stable. All other shuttles are reporting ready for pre-flight systems check on schedule."**

The minutes tick by in the relative quiet of the cockpit, punctuated by the occasional crackle of routine hangar control chatter over Kael's headset and the low hum of the *Void Skimmer's* powered-up systems. Wes can feel the thrum of the ship beneath him, a beast ready to be unleashed. Through the viewports, the activity in Hangar C-7 continues at a brisk pace, ground crews beginning to make their final disconnections from the other two expedition shuttles.

The chronometer on Wes's HUD reads 13:42.

Just then, a figure is seen darting through the organized chaos near the *Void Skimmer's* open ramp. It's Marina, moving with a speed and agility that belies her current condition, her Jedi reflexes allowing her to weave expertly around a slow-moving cargo lifter and a preoccupied ground crew member without breaking stride. She carries her datapad clutched securely in one hand, her expression a mixture of focused determination and perhaps a slight breathlessness from her haste.

Wes went through the logs and listened in on the comm chatter.   He could sense the curiosity and the underlying tension many on the ground crews were feeling.  One wouldn’t know it to look at them.  They all moved with purpose doing their duty making sure that all three shuttles were prepped and ready to fly.   


Wes looked at his chrono several times.    13:35 came and passed as did 13:40.   He resisted the urge to reach out to sense where she was.   He knew her and how she felt about performing her duties.  The last thing she needed was him ‘reaching out’ to see where she was.   Standing up from the copilot's seat.  He looked out and scanned the area.  Dozens of people moved about. Many were tending to  the *Void Skimmer.*  


Looking at his chronometer once more it read 13:42.  He took a breath and let it out.   As he did so he spotted movement moving towards the *Skimmer.*  The figure darted back and forth and Wes immediately recognized the form and its movement patterns.   It was Marina and she had made it with minutes to spare.   **”There she is.”**  He said as he raised his hand and pointed at her as she weaved in and out of two hydraulic load lifters.   Despite her pregnancy she was still moving effectively and quickly.  


Seeing her reach the gangplank he nodded at Kael.  **”Inform Control that all crew is aboard.”** He turned and moved for the ladder to head down.   **”I’ll go see what she got for us.  I’ll be back by 13:55.”**

Kael, who had been monitoring the final countdown sequence from Hangar Control and running a last diagnostic sweep on the Void Skimmer's thruster output, glances up as Wes points out Marina's approach. He gives a curt nod. "**Acknowledged. Control, *Void Skimmer*. All Team Gamma personnel are now aboard or accounted for at the shuttle. Standing by for final launch clearance.**" He relays the message with practiced efficiency, his gaze already flicking back to his instruments, ensuring every system is nominal for the imminent departure. He doesn't comment on Marina's timing, his focus entirely on the operational readiness of his vessel.

Marina hurries up the *Void Skimmer's* ramp, her breath coming in slight gasps, a flush on her cheeks from the exertion of navigating the bustling hangar bay and the damaged corridors of the *Chu'unthor II* to make it on time. She carries a well-worn datapad clutched in one hand, its screen displaying a complex, ancient-looking schematic. As the last one aboard, she immediately turns to the ramp controls. She punches the sequence to retract and seal the ramp. The hydraulic hiss and the solid thud of the ramp locking into place echo through the aft compartment. 

She leans back against the newly sealed hatch for a moment, catching her breath, just as Wes descends the ladder from the cockpit area into the main hold. Her gaze meets his, a mixture of relief at making it, apology for her timing, and the underlying excitement of her archival findings. 

**"Cutting it a bit close, I know,"** she says, her voice a little breathless. **"The Archives... Archivist Ree is meticulously slow, and Master Vantos wanted to discuss a few cross-references. But I got it."** She holds up the datapad triumphantly, a tired but satisfied smile on her face. **"Or at least, I got *something*."**

Coming down the ladder Wes could hear the ramp closing.  Looking to the entry point he could see Marina leaning against the bulkhead.  Her chest rising and falling quickly as she was catching her breath from the exertion of making it on time.  He looked at her.  He could sense and see her relief and excitement that radiated off her.   Walking towards her he listened as she explained what had taken so long.  He smiled beneath his helmet as she held up the datapad.   Her voice was excited with what she had found.   **”Good.  Work with Sera and start going over it.  Get any info that Kael can use to him first.  If there’s anything teams Alpha and Beta can use, get it to them.  Then going over what we will need on the freighter.  Good work.  We have about 15 minutes until mission launch.   Get strapped in.  It’s looking to be a bumpy ride.”**

Marina nods, her breathing already steadying as she pushes herself off the bulkhead. The professional focus returns to her eyes, overriding the lingering fatigue. **"Understood, Wes. Sera and I will get right on it."** She glances towards the main hold where Sera set up earlier. **"I'll link up with her immediately. Any critical navigational data or structural weak points relevant to Kael's approach will be our first priority to extract and relay."**

She gives a quick, appreciative nod for his confidence in her findings. **"Fifteen minutes. Right."** The thought of a "bumpy ride" through the Graveyard Shallows, as Kael had described it, brings a flicker of apprehension, but it's quickly overshadowed by the task at hand. She clutches the datapad with its precious, ancient information. **"I'll find a secure spot and get strapped in once Sera and I have a preliminary plan for data dissemination. Let's hope this 'something' gives us an edge."**

With a final, determined look at Wes, Marina turns and heads towards the main hold area, already calling out, **"Sera? I found some archival fragments. Let's see if we can make sense of this before Kael throws us into a blender..."** Her voice trails off as she moves deeper into the shuttle, ready to dive into the technical challenge.

Wes nodded when she said she would find a secure spot and strap in.   **”Good work Rin.”**  He said quietly over the din of the noises of the shuttle.   **”I know you will be going over the info but I hope you all can rest some before we get into the thick of it on the derelict.  Let me know what you find and I’ll let Kael to expect something that’s hopefully useful.”**  He saw her nod before she turned to where she was going to set up near Sera.   


Turning he made his way back to the cockpits access ladder.  Stepping onto the deck he sat in the copilot's chair.  **”Marina has some info.  She and Sera are going over it now.  You will be the first one to get info pertaining to the target and approach.”**  He said as he started going over his controls.   At 13:50 he opened the ship's speaker system.   ***”Ten minutes until launch.  Strap in if you haven’t”***

As Marina passes Wes on her way to the main hold to work with Sera, she hears his quiet words of encouragement and concern. She gives his armored arm a quick, affectionate squeeze, a silent "thank you" that speaks volumes of her appreciation for his thoughtfulness amidst the pre-mission tension. The small gesture conveys her understanding and her own readiness for the task ahead. She then continues into the hold, her focus already shifting to the datapad and the challenge of deciphering its ancient secrets with Sera.

Kael glances over from his pre-flight checks as Wes re-enters the cockpit and settles into the co-pilot's seat. He gives a noncommittal grunt at the news about Marina and Sera working on new intel. **"Good. Any edge we can get in that scrap yard is welcome. Just make sure it's actionable, not academic."** His fingers continue to move across his console, verifying final system readings. **"If they find anything that changes my approach vectors or highlights unexpected energy signatures around the *Shadow*, patch it through directly. Otherwise, I'm sticking to Korta's plotted ingress until we have visual."**

He acknowledges Wes's ten-minute call with a slight inclination of his head, his attention already divided between his instruments and the external comms from Hangar Control, which are starting their final countdown sequence for the coordinated departure of all three expedition shuttles. The low thrum of the *Void Skimmer's* engines begins to deepen slightly as Kael brings them to full standby power.

Throughout the *Void Skimmer*, the remaining team members hear Wes's announcement over the internal address system. Jannsen and Echo, already having stowed their gear and settled into the cramped acceleration couches or crash webbing lining the bulkheads of the main hold, give their restraints a final, decisive tug, their expressions set and professional.

Further forward, Marina and Sera, huddled over their datapads, pause their intense technical discussion. The glow of ancient schematics and lines of decryption code reflect in their focused eyes. With practiced efficiency, they secure their datapads – magnetically clamped to the workstation Sera has claimed and then ensure their own harnesses are cinched tight, bracing for the turbulent ride Kael has all but guaranteed. The atmosphere inside the blastboat becomes taut with anticipation, the low, powerful hum of the engines a promise of imminent departure into the hazardous unknown of the Karthon Expanse.

Wes nodded as he sent a message to Marina and Sera.  **”Copy that.   Actionable and patched through directly.”**  Wes quickly composed the message and sent it off to the two women.    


He watched the chrono as he listened to the comms chatter and looked over the ships systems.   Between himself and Kael, whose hands were a blur across the controls they had things covered. When the chrono ticked off 13:55 he opened the ship's speaker once again.   ***”Five minutes until launch”***
The hangar bay comms crackle with the clipped, professional voices of Hangar Control coordinating the departures.

**"Team Alpha, *Pathfinder*, you are clear for umbilical detach and mag-lock release. Proceed to exit vector zero-niner-zero. Good hunting."**
Moments later, the *Void Skimmer's* internal sensors would register the slight pressure change as the massive hangar bay doors begin to cycle for the next shuttle.

**"Team Beta, *Healer's Hope*, umbilical detach and mag-lock release confirmed. You are clear. Proceed to exit vector zero-niner-five. May the Force be with you."**

The *Void Skimmer* gives a slight lurch as its own docking clamps disengage with a series of thuds that resonate through the deck plating.
**"Team Gamma, *Void Skimmer*, you are clear for umbilical detach. Mag-locks releasing. Stand by for exit vector... You are clear to proceed, exit vector one-zero-zero. Good luck, *Skimmer*."**

**"Acknowledged, Hangar Control,"** Kael's voice is calm, all business. **"Del-Fin, all secure aft?"** He doesn't wait for a verbal reply, his hands already moving, easing the throttle forward. The *Void Skimmer's* engines roar to life with a deeper, more powerful vibration.

The shuttle lifts smoothly from its berth, the interior lights dimming slightly as power is shunted to the primary thrusters. Through the cockpit viewports, the vast, brightly lit expanse of Hangar Bay C-7 slides away, replaced by the star-dusted black of space and the immense, scarred hull of the *Chu'unthor II* receding below them. Kael expertly guides the blastboat along the designated departure vector, clearing the Praxeum ship's local interference field.

**"Clear of the *Chu'unthor*,"** Kael announces a few minutes later, his voice tight with concentration as he navigates through the orbital ballet of the small Altisian flotilla. **"Initiating coordinates for micro-jump to primary hyperspace entry point. Stand by for jump."**

The *Void Skimmer* shudders for a moment as the short-range navicomputer locks onto the coordinates. Then, with a lurch that presses everyone back into their acceleration couches, the stars outside elongate into streaks of light, and the familiar ethereal tunnel of a micro-jump envelops them. The journey is brief, perhaps only fifteen minutes, a necessary hop to clear the immediate gravitational complexities of Karthos Prime and its attendant debris before engaging the main hyperdrive.

When they revert to realspace, the view is different. The Karthos system is a distant jewel behind them. Ahead, the dense, swirling clouds of the Lesser Karthon Nebula loom, shot through with unsettling pockets of darkness and faint, unidentifiable energy signatures.

**"Micro-jump complete,"** Kael reports, his hands already flying across the console, inputting the much longer sequence for "Korta's Passage." **"Primary hyperdrive charging. All systems nominal for main jump. Strap in tight, folks. Korta's Passage is not known for its smooth rides."** He glances at his console. **"Engaging hyperdrive in three... two... one..."**

With a powerful surge and a disorienting flash, the stars outside stretch into the iconic blue-white lines of hyperspace. The *Void Skimmer* plunges into the interstellar void, beginning its nearly four-hour journey towards the Graveyard Shallows and the unknown derelict that awaits them. The low thrum of the hyperdrive is a constant, monotonous hum, a stark contrast to the tension that still fills the cramped interior of the blastboat.

When the *Skimmer* detached Kael asked about the others being secure.   Almost before he could give the pilot a thumbs up Kael had  pushed the throttles forward pushing them all into their respective spots.  


A short time later after the micro jump dropped them at the coordinates where the main jump would start.  Kael was already on it as he began interning the sequence for the jump.   To make sure all was green with the ship's systems Wes scanned the instruments waiting for the countdown to begin.  Having nothing to report he held on for the ride as Kael counted down and the stars to stretch.   


Once the ship jumped Wes checked the systems.   All is green across the board.  **”Four hours everyone.  Marina, Sera, how's it going?”**

*(Over the internal comms, her voice clear despite the hyperdrive's hum)* ***"We're making some headway. The archival data is dense, but I've isolated some partial schematics for an ancient vessel class – a 'Nomad Class Void-Barge' – that shares some general mass and configuration similarities with the *Veiled Shadow's* long-range profile. It's giving us a potential starting point for likely internal layouts. Sera's helping me cross-reference some of its listed security protocols with known archaic systems. When you get a moment, could you come aft to the main hold? It would be easier to show you on the larger display here what we're piecing together."***

***"Yeah, Wes. Some of these old security measures are... quaint, but others look like they could still be a nasty surprise if they have any residual power. Definitely worth a look before we get too close."*** Her tone is professional and focused.

Wes listened as Marina and Sera relayed back what they had found so far with the archived data that had been found.   They felt it significant enough to call him back to look it over.   **”Copy.   I’ll make my way to you.”** He said before turning off the mic.    


**”I’ll be back, Kael.  If you need a breather for a few let me know.   I’ll see what they have found and check in with Ror and Echo.   If something rears it ugly head let us know.”**  He gave the pilot a nod and started his descent down the ladder.   


On approach to the two Wes gave each a nod.  **”What do you have? 
“**  The armored Mandalorian took the open seat near Sera and secured himself in case of turbulence.  So far the mission has started off well.   Any useful info they had come across would hopefully continue the trend.

Marina looks up as Wes approaches and takes the seat, gratitude in her eyes for his quick response. She taps her datapad, and the small fold-down table between their acceleration couches, currently displaying Sera's complex code, flickers and then projects a clearer, though still fragmented, holographic image of the "Nomad Class Void-Barge" schematics she'd mentioned.

**"Alright, take a look at this,"** Marina begins, gesturing to the holo. **"This is the most complete general layout we could find for a vessel of roughly the *Veiled Shadow's* suspected size and pre-Republic dating. As you can see, massive central cargo bays are standard, which is good for Gaxx's shopping list."** She highlights a section. **"Primary engineering seems consistently aft-located, heavily shielded. The bridge or command structure is typically forward and elevated, as expected."**

She then zooms in on a more detailed, though still incomplete, internal section. **"What's interesting, and potentially useful for us, are these recurring access points. See these junctions? They appear to be primary data core access hubs or main system conduits, usually located deep within the protected mid-section, often near what would have been crew or command habitation areas. If the *Veiled Shadow* follows a similar design philosophy, these could be our best bet for finding intact nav-computer components or even ship-wide schematics once we're aboard."**

Sera leans in, tapping another section of the holo-display with her datapad stylus, bringing up an overlay of what look like archaic security symbols and flowcharts. **"And from a security perspective,"** Sera adds, **"these 'Void-Barges' often relied on layered physical and basic energy grid defenses around such critical areas. Think pressure plates, localized stun fields, or even automated sentry turrets in key corridors, rather than sophisticated network intrusion countermeasures. Their idea of a firewall was probably a meter of reinforced durasteel."** She grins faintly. **"If any of that is still powered, it'll be crude but potentially dangerous if we stumble into it blindly. Knowing these common chokepoints and likely defensive placements from Marina's find helps us plan our approach vectors and what to look for with initial scans."**

Marina nods in agreement. **"Exactly. It's not a perfect match for the *Veiled Shadow*, obviously, but it gives us a better search pattern than just wandering aimlessly through a kilometers-long derelict. And,"** she adds, her expression turning a fraction more serious, **"I also found a very fragmented survey log from an ancient Jedi Pathfinder team. It mentions encountering a 'leviathan ghost ship' in a sector with gravitic anomalies similar to the Karthon Expanse. The external description is a hazy match for the *Shadow*."** She pulls up a corrupted text file on her datapad, showing mostly garbled symbols with a few discernible words. **"The log is mostly unreadable, but it clearly warns of 'silent guardians' – likely those automated defenses Sera mentioned – and there's a repeated, almost frantic reference to 'whispers that unsettle the metal' before the log cuts off abruptly. No idea what that means, but it suggests something beyond standard system malfunctions."**

Wes looked at the holo as Marina and Sera ran through their findings.   There was some good intel there that would benefit them.   Even if the ships weren't an exact match and only similar, the benefits outweigh the negatives.   The centralized cargo bays and the primary data core access points were a plus, if they were there or close by. 


The possible security profile as laid out by Sera was a bit concerning if there was still any power going to the defensive systems.  If there was still power perhaps they could be accessed and shut down or used in their favor. 


**”Alright, we can try to locate one of these access points.  Early on.   If we can get some accurate schematics of the ship all the better.   If we can’t we rely on what we have.   Also if those access points can get you access to the  ships defenses, shut them down and or make them work for us.   We don’t know what or who we will find there.   Let’s take all the advantages we can.  As for the cargo bays they will be priority.  Get there as quickly and safely as possible.  Having an idea of what we may run across will help that.”**

Reaching out to the holo display he swiped to the display to bring the logs Marina had found.   Despite being corrupted like she said he read them over for himself.  The mention of ‘silent guardians’ and ‘whispers that unsettle the metal’ caused him to reread that part twice.   That it came from a Jedi Pathfinder team made him wonder if it had anything to do with the Waypoint.  It seemed like too much of a coincidence.   The change in his dreams the night before hearing about leading the mission.   He knew the two next to him could pick up on things.  Especially Marina due to their bond.   **”Jedi Pathfinder team that talks about ‘silent guardians’ and ‘whispers that unsettle the metal’.  Could their encounters be Force related?  A person or individuals.  Perhaps an object?”**  He turned his helmeted head towards Marina.   The mention of the object obviously in relation to the Wayfinder.

Marina listens intently to Wes's theory, her brow furrowed in thought as she considers the cryptic phrases from the Pathfinder log. The connection he's drawing between the ancient derelict and the Wayfinder is a heavy implication, one she approaches with her usual blend of Jedi insight and cautious pragmatism.

**"Jedi Pathfinders..."** she begins slowly, her gaze distant for a moment as she accesses her own knowledge. **"They were a unique branch of the Order, Wes, often operating far from the Core, exploring uncharted regions and seeking out lost knowledge or forgotten Force vergences. They were known for their resilience and deep connection to the Living Force, sometimes encountering phenomena that defied easy explanation by more... traditional Jedi."**

She looks back at him, her expression serious. **"'Silent guardians' could certainly refer to ancient automated defenses, perhaps even droids of a technology we don't recognize. But coming from a Pathfinder log, the term could also imply something more... attuned. Constructs imbued with the Force, or even localized Force manifestations they couldn't fully comprehend."**

Her frown deepens slightly at the phrase "whispers that unsettle the metal." **"That's more unusual. It could be a highly specific energy field, some kind of resonant frequency that affects technology in strange ways, or yes, potentially something directly interacting with the Force within objects or even the ship's structure itself. Pathfinders often sought out places strong in the Force, and sometimes those places, or the artifacts found within them, had... unpredictable qualities."**

She doesn't directly address the unspoken link to the Wayfinder in Wes's eyes, but the possibility hangs in the air. **"If the *Veiled Shadow* was a site visited by Pathfinders, and they encountered something potent enough to make them record such warnings before their log cut off... we need to be exceptionally vigilant. It could be environmental, technological, or, as you suggest, intrinsically tied to the Force."**

Sera, listening to this exchange, raises an eyebrow. Her expertise lies in circuits and code, not the esoteric nuances of the Force. **"Force-related guardians? Or an object that 'whispers' to metal?"** She shakes her head, a slight smile playing on her lips. **"That's a bit outside my usual diagnostic parameters. If it's not putting out an EM field I can read or a data stream I can tap, I'm relying on you two Force-sensitives to flag it before it tries to turn the *Void Skimmer's* hull into abstract art."** She looks from Wes to Marina with a mixture of professional respect and perhaps a touch of Zeltron amusement at the Jedi's more mystical concerns. **"Just give me a heads-up if the ship starts getting 'feelings', alright?"**

Wes listened as Marina talked.  It was evident in how she spoke that she had some knowledge about the Pathfinders.   This was one of the times he wished he had a bit more of that ‘Jedi knowledge’ that many who started their training sooner had.  He didn’t though and what he brought to the table was a different set of skills that complimented Marinas’ quite well.   He gave her a small nod as he thought.  **”All the more reason to get in and out as quickly as possible.  Thanks for that rundown.  I hope there’s no surprises in store for us but I think we all know better.”**  His gaze took the two of them in.   


Wes looked at Sera when she spoke up about their Force related topic.   Her smile, infectious as it was, made him smile too as she asked for a heads up if something Force related was going on.   **”Copy that Sera.  If anything happens outside the normal realm and either of us senses something the whole team will be notified.”**  He looked at Marina and nodded before looking back at the Zeltron.  **”That goes for you too.  You’ve already shown me you have some empathetic sense.  You may sense something we don’t”**

Sera's smile widens at Wes's acknowledgment of her empathetic abilities. **"Noted, Wes. My 'people skills' sometimes pick up on a different kind of static than your Force senses, that's for sure."** She gives a playful wink. **"If the ship's bulkheads start whispering sweet nothings to me, or if I get a sudden urge to redecorate the engine room with mynock pelts, I'll be sure to let you know something's... off-kilter."** Her tone is light, but there's an underlying assurance that she'll be alert to any unusual emotional or atmospheric shifts. **"Seriously though, teamwork and open comms will be key. We all bring different sensors to the party."**

**”Mynock pelts?  Really?”** He said with a chuckle at her comment before his demeanor once again turned serious.   He looked back at the holo screen they had been looking at.  **”Good work both of you.   Keep digging and give me an update at 60 minute intervals.  Share this and future intel with the others so they can be up to date.  If you find something that requires my attention let me know.”**   Undoing the safety gear he stood and jerked a thumb in the direction of Roric and Echo.  **”I’ll check on them before heading back up with Kael.”**  Standing he gave Marina a nod and reached out to her with the Force.   She could feel the confidence he felt in her and in the work she and Sera were both doing in finding the edge they were looking for.  


Quickly he moved to where Roric and Echo were seated.   Roric, not to the Mandalorians surprise, looked to be sleeping.  Wes smiled knowing that the soldier, like himself, knew when to rest.  Wes gave Echo a nod as he sat down and strapped in.  **”How are you two?   I wanted to go over operations and get an idea as to what you’re thinking.   I’ve asked Marina and Sera to keep you both in the loop with what info Marina found in the archives.  They are going through it now and you should receive the first packet soon.”**  As if on queue his bracer computer vibrated and he could see the message from Sera with an attached file.

Echo looks up as Wes approaches, her expression neutral but attentive. She had been quietly observing the hold, her gaze periodically sweeping their surroundings, even while seated. She gives Wes a slight nod as he sits.

**"Systems nominal on our end, Wes,"** she replies, her voice calm and even. **"Gear is stowed and checked. We're ready."** She acknowledges the incoming data from Sera with a subtle glance at her own bracer. **"Good. Any intel on the target's layout or potential internal hazards will help us plan entry points and overwatch positions more effectively."**

She considers for a moment, her gaze thoughtful. **"Standard procedure for us would be to establish a secure perimeter at the initial breach or docking point. Roric can provide heavy cover while I do a primary sweep of the immediate vicinity – looking for structural instability, signs of recent activity, or immediate automated threats. From there, we can leapfrog forward, securing sections as your tech team advances. If it's a maze of corridors, as these old freighters often are, clear communication on secured sectors will be vital to prevent friendly fire or getting cut off."**

Jannsen, who had indeed appeared to be resting with his eyes closed, opens them as Wes speaks, instantly alert. There's no grogginess, only the focused attention of a veteran soldier. He listens to Echo's summary and then adds his own gruff assessment.

**"She's right. Beachhead first. Then controlled advance."** He glances at Wes. **"My primary role will be to lay down suppressive fire if we hit active resistance and cover any necessary retreats. The ion rifles will be useful if we're dealing with droids. I'll take one, Echo can take the other if she's comfortable with it, or we keep it as a team asset. Grenades for breaching or area denial."** He pauses, then asks directly, **"What's your preferred method for room clearing and dealing with potential ambushes, Wes? Standard slice-the-pie, or something more... dynamic?"** His question is practical, seeking to understand his new leader's tactical approach.

He listened to his scout and weapons support give their plans and expectations for moving through the space hulk.  Without knowing what to expect, what they laid out was the best approach.  In response to Roric’s question about room clearing he nodded.   **”We will have to tackle that on a room by room basis.   Until we know otherwise, slice the pie.   If we have known hostiles in the room or corridor then we evaluate and possibly take that dynamic approach and move in.”**  Seeing nods from both he followed up.  **”Knowing if something is around the corner or beyond the door should be too bad.    Marina and I can sense anything living and the void in the Force from a droid.  So big surprises shouldn’t be a problem.   It’s the little ones we will have to worry about.”**  He looked at Echo.  **”As for the second ion rifle. If you don’t want it I can take it.  I know you will be scouting ahead.  If someone needs it I can pass it off. Or make use of it.”**

Echo considers Wes's offer of the second ion rifle, her expression thoughtful. **"I usually travel light for initial recon, prioritize mobility and stealth over heavy firepower. My carbine is versatile enough for most initial contacts."** She glances at Roric. **"If Roric's comfortable with the primary ion rifle, I can manage with standard blasters and grenades for suppressive or disruptive effect if needed during a sweep. However,"** she adds, meeting Wes's gaze again, **"having a second ion rifle readily available with the main group, perhaps with you or Marina if she's not actively on tech, makes tactical sense. We can reassess if the primary threat turns out to be heavily mechanized."** She gives a slight nod, indicating her flexibility on the matter.

Her brow furrows slightly as Wes mentions sensing living beings or the 'void' of droids through the Force. This is clearly outside her operational experience. **"Sensing... life? And droids?"** she repeats, not with disbelief, but with a professional curiosity, trying to integrate this new, somewhat abstract tactical information. **"Alright. That's... a variable we don't usually have. We'll rely on your read for that, then. Our scanners can pick up energy signatures and motion, but distinguishing between a dormant maintenance droid and an active security unit at range, or sensing a hidden organic threat... that's new. We'll adapt."**

Jannsen listens to Wes's explanation of their Force-based detection capabilities with a stoic, unreadable expression. He's a man who trusts what he can see, hear, and what his weapon can reliably hit. The concept of sensing 'voids' or 'life' through an invisible energy field is likely far removed from his practical combat experience.

He grunts. **"So, you Jedi can... feel 'em out before we kick in the door. That's... efficient, if it works."** There's no sarcasm, just a soldier's pragmatic assessment of a new, unfamiliar asset. **"Good. Reduces the chance of walking into a killzone blind. As for the ion rifles,"** he nods towards Echo, then Wes. **"I'll take point with the first one. If Del-Fin carries the second, it gives us tactical flexibility. If Echo needs it on point, we can pass it up."** He settles back slightly, having processed the immediate tactical implications, his mind likely already running through engagement scenarios.

Wes nodded as he saw Echo process and talk through what he said and out sensing life and droids.  **”Droids are a little different than life.  All living things have a signature in the Force.   Droids and nonliving things do not.   It’s more difficult with those things as they create a void.   I may not know exactly what it is but if it’s moving it’s something to take note of and yes it’s an advantage we will make use of.”** 


As Roric added in his thoughts Wes smiled.   Not long ago he thought a lot like the veteran soldier did.  He knew the Jedi had extra senses he could never explain.  It wasn’t until Marina, who pulled the veil from his eyes, opened up a different galaxy to him.  **”Every tactical advantage we have will be put to use.”**  He looked between both and saw their nods.   **”One thing, you both have been at this a long time.  You wouldn’t be here if you weren’t very good at what you do.  I know what it’s like to have a gut feeling.   Rely on that as well.  Copy?”**

Echo gives a sharp, affirmative nod at Wes's explanation and his trust in their experience. **"Copy, Wes. Gut feelings are often just experience processed faster than conscious thought. We'll keep our senses sharp – all of them."** The way Wes explains the Force detection, while still abstract to her, is presented as a practical tool, and she seems to accept it on that basis.

Jannsen offers a rare, almost imperceptible upturn at the corner of his mouth, perhaps a Trandoshan equivalent of a acknowledging smile, or simply a sign he appreciates the directness and the respect Wes is showing their experience. **"Loud and clear, Del-Fin. Experience counts. We'll call out anything that doesn't feel right."** He gives his own gear a subtle, reassuring pat, the professional soldier ready for the unknown.

A comfortable, professional understanding seems to settle between Wes and his two security specialists. They've established a baseline for tactical communication and acknowledged the unique blend of conventional experience and Force-sensitivity their team brings. The *Void Skimmer* continues its steady journey through hyperspace, the minutes ticking down towards their arrival in the hazardous Graveyard Shallows.

Wes smiled and nodded at Roric.  The man’s confidence in his and Echo’s experience and training was evident.   It raised his confidence in working with two unknowns.  **”Sounds good.   I know I don’t need to tell either one of you this but get some sleep while you can.  It will be in short supply rather quickly.”**  He gave them a nod as he unhooked his restraints and made his way back up to the cockpit.


Ascending the ladder Wes could hear the faint chimes coming from some of the computers.   Looking at the nav screen as he cleared the deck he saw that they were still on course and still on schedule.   He moved to the secondary seat as he looked at Kael.   The pilot was still working looking over some navi equations.    **”If you want to relax for a few before we get into the mix let me know.”**   He tapped a few keys and started looking over the schematics Marina and Sera had been going over.

As Wes stands and makes his way from the main hold, he sees that Marina has indeed found a relatively quiet spot on one of the fold-down acceleration couches further aft. She's strapped in securely, her datapad resting dark in her lap, and her eyes are closed, her breathing deep and even. Her Force presence feels calm and centered, clearly having taken his earlier advice to rest and prepare inwardly for the challenges ahead. Sera, meanwhile, is still diligently working at the terminal, her brow furrowed in concentration as she sifts through the archaic data Marina had uncovered, occasionally muttering technical jargon under her breath.

Kael glances up briefly as Wes re-enters the cockpit and settles into the co-pilot's seat, acknowledging his presence with a slight nod before returning his attention to a complex astrogation projection on his main screen. It shows "Korta's Passage" – the narrow, recently charted hyperspace route – with multiple potential variance calculations flickering beside it.

**"Appreciate the offer, Wes,"** Kael replies, his voice focused, not taking his eyes off the display. **"But I'm good. Running final trajectory verifications for our exit vector into the Graveyard Shallows. Korta's data is solid, but this passage has a few... 'unpredictable' energy currents according to her notes. Better to have all the variables nailed down before we drop out of hyperspace into that mess."** He makes a minute adjustment on his controls. **"Besides, someone needs to make sure this old bird doesn't decide to take an unscheduled detour into a black hole while everyone else is napping or decrypting ancient star-whispers."** There's no real bite to his words, just the dry pragmatism of a pilot who trusts his own vigilance above all else. He taps another control. **"Schematics any good? Anything in there look like it'll make my approach easier, or harder?"**

Wes nodded as Kael declined the chance to rest, wanting to keep an eye on things.  **”I understand wanting to see it through.”**  He managed to get out before he was asked if any intel had been found out.  


Smiling beneath his helmet he hit a few buttons and brought up the ship Marina and Sera had shown him earlier. The ships image spun in a slow circle    **”Now this isn’t the *Veiled Shadow* but it fits what we know based on the size and pre republic dating.  It’s a ‘Nomad class Void Barge.’   It should have multiple larger interior cargo and or hanger bays.”** 


He let the image spin for a second before continuing.   **”I already spoke with Roric and Echo.  They are all set on setting up a beachhead so to speak when we touch down.  If it’s similar to this here,”**  Wes pointed at the slowly spinning freighter.  **”The main cargo bays should be a main priority for us.   Marina and Echo were looking for places where we may be able to find the other high priority equipment and info.   Which leads me to you.   Where do you want to be when we touch down?   I know we are more than likely going to have a rough entry.  You want to stay with the *Skimmer* and make sure she’s good to go for the return trip?”**

Kael studies the rotating holographic schematic of the "Nomad Class Void-Barge" with a pilot's critical eye, taking in the projected internal hangar bays. **"Interior bays, huh? Ambitious design for something that old. Means a solid hull breach is the only way in if the main doors are sealed or unpowered, unless Sera finds a back door on their external network – assuming they even *had* an external network that's still accessible."**

He considers Wes's question about his role after touchdown. **"You're right, entry into the Shallows and any landing or docking with that derelict is going to be sporty. My priority one is getting us there in one piece and then ensuring the *Void Skimmer* remains operational and ready for immediate extraction. This isn't a pleasure cruise where I can just power down and join the away team for a stroll."**

He taps a control on his console, bringing up the *Skimmer's* energy reserve readouts. **"If we're docked inside, I'll need to keep essential systems on standby – shields at minimum, sensors active for any external threats, engines ready for a hot start. If we're forced to EVA or use a breached hull section for entry, I'll need to maintain station-keeping in a debris field, which is a full-time job in itself."**

He glances at Wes. **"So, yes. I'm staying with the *Skimmer*. I'm your ride in, and more importantly, your ride out. You and your team focus on the salvage. I'll focus on making sure you *have* a way to bring it home."** He gives a grim, almost non-existent smile. **"Besides, someone needs to be ready to blast us out if that 'leviathan ghost ship' decides it doesn't appreciate visitors poking around its guts."**

Wes smiled as Kael finally cracked the faintest of smiles.  He had been thinking along the same lines.   The *Skimmer* was going to need tending to and Kael was the best one to do it.  **”Good.  We’ll round up the goods you get us and them home.  Do what you need to.   Anything or anyone surprises you, get on the horn.”** 


Wes turned back to the co-pilots station.   And started going over any new info that Marina and Sera had sent.  Looking at the chrono they still had some time until things got interesting.

The hyperspace journey stretches on, marked by the monotonous thrum of the *Void Skimmer’s* engines. Kael, a statue of focused concentration, occasionally makes minute adjustments to their course through "Korta's Passage," his eyes constantly scanning the complex data streams on his console.

At the co-pilot station, Wes delves into the steady flow of information from Marina and Sera. Their combined efforts paint a slightly less opaque picture of what might await them.

The "Nomad Class Void-Barge" schematics, while ancient and generalized, offer some tantalizing possibilities. These colossal freighters, Marina's notes suggest, were often built with significant system redundancy. Access points to critical areas like engineering or potential data cores – likely deep within the vessel's protected heart – might have multiple approaches. A large, primary ventral hangar seems to be a recurring design feature, offering Kael a potential, if risky, initial point of contact. Marina has also flagged historical weak points common to this class – typically around primary thruster assemblies or archaic reactor cores, areas that would demand extreme caution.

Sera’s analysis of the fragmented Pathfinder log adds a more ominous layer. The "silent guardian" attacks, she’s pieced together, weren't random. They seemed to activate when the ancient Jedi team tried to delve deeper, beyond general cargo areas, or when they attempted to interface with specific ship systems. Before these encounters, the log mentions "archaic energy signatures" or "dormant system activation," strongly implying these guardians are technological. The more cryptic phrase, "whispers that unsettle the metal," Sera theorizes, might be a less technical description of a powerful, unusual electromagnetic field. Such an emission could certainly wreak havoc on droid functions and, if strong enough, might even affect the *Skimmer’s* systems or their own gear on close approach.

The picture forming in Wes’s mind is one of a colossal, ancient derelict, potentially a maze internally, with specific, heavily protected core sections housing their primary salvage targets. Entry will likely be via a ventral bay, demanding immediate security assessment by Echo and Roric. And woven through it all is the unsettling possibility of dormant, technologically advanced guardians and an unknown environmental or energy hazard. The data from Marina and Sera provides focus, but also underscores the immense risks.

Wes looked over the info that was trickling in from Sera and Marina.   The two women had been working hard.  Marina has obviously finished her quiet respite that he saw her taking a bit ago.   The intro was good. There were some interesting possibilities that could make things much easier for them.   Yet while there was some good there was also some concerning issues.  


Sera’s findings were more on the ship's possible defensive capabilities that she dug out of the Pathfinders log.   If this ship was the same ship or something similar they were going to have to be very cautious the deeper they went into the derelict leviathan.  They were all going to have to be on point. It was good that he had requested the ion weapons.   From the looks of it they would prove invaluable on the mission.  


He started looking at the ventral bay where they would most likely make entry.  He thought of his father who had taught him basic and advanced tactics since he was just a boy.   He began using that knowledge to make a plan.

Wes absorbs the compiled data from Marina and Sera, the holographic display before him cycling through fragmented schematics and Sera's highlighted security concerns. The ancient "Nomad Class Void-Barge" design, with its massive ventral hangar, offers a clear, if potentially hazardous, primary point of entry. His mind, honed by years of Mandalorian tactical training under his father and now augmented by his growing Jedi senses, begins to layer potential strategies over the ghostly blueprints.

The intelligence about the "silent guardians" activating upon deeper intrusion or system interface is critical. It suggests a tiered defense – perhaps the outer cargo bays are relatively inert, but any attempt to reach vital systems like engineering or the data core will trigger a response. Sera's theory about an EM field capable of unsettling droids and metal is also a significant concern; it could disable their own tech, or worse, affect the *Void Skimmer* itself if they get too close to the source.

The path to the primary objectives – the hyperdrive coils, nav-matrix stabilizers, and power converters Gaxx needs – likely lies deep within the *Veiled Shadow*. Reaching them will require a methodical advance, securing areas section by section.

Kael, noticing Wes's intense focus on the schematics, glances over. **"Find a good spot to park this rust bucket, Del-Fin?"** he asks, his tone dry, though his eyes show professional interest. **"That ventral bay looks like our best bet, assuming it's not structurally compromised or a welcome mat for whatever's still ticking inside."**

Drawing on his tactical knowledge, Wes visualizes the approach. The ventral hangar, if accessible, is vast. Landing the *Void Skimmer* towards one end, perhaps near a secondary access corridor if visible on approach, would offer a more defensible initial position than being exposed in the center. Echo and Roric would need to secure the immediate ramp area and any adjacent corridors instantly. From there, a leapfrogging advance: Echo scouting, Roric providing cover, while Wes, Marina, and Sera form the core element, assessing and salvaging.

The "silent guardians" are the primary X-factor. If they are technological, the ion weapons will be key. A direct assault might be costly. Perhaps Sera can identify their power source or control system if they can get close enough to an interface point *before* triggering a full alert. The "whispers that unsettle the metal" – that’s a wild card. If it’s an EM field, they'll need to identify its source and effective range quickly. Marina's diagnostic tools and Wes's own Force senses will be crucial for that.

The thought of a tiered defense suggests a strategy: initial insertion into a seemingly safe peripheral area (like a main cargo bay accessed from the ventral hangar), establish a secure base of operations, then methodical, cautious probes towards the deeper, more critical sections. They'll need to be prepared to fall back to the *Skimmer* quickly if things go sideways. The primary objectives are vital, but so is the survival of the team.

**”I think so.”**  Wes answered Kael’s question about finding a landing space.   **”If the *Shadow* is at all similar to what we’ve been looking at then there should be a massive ventral hanger.”**  Wes hit a button and brought the picture up of what they were expecting to find.  **”Here.”**  He said as he pointed.  **”This is looking to be the best place to set down.  Providing it's accessible and not in shambles once we access it.   Based on some info found on a Jedi Pathfinder team from that time there should be little to no defenses in the hangar bay.   Defenses become more dense and dangerous the deeper into the ship one goes.  Protecting critical and valuable cargo and or data.  Off to one of the ends would be the better point to set down.  Provides some cover instead of being in the middle of the bay open to all sides.”** 


He was quiet for a moment as he looked at the potential access points that led from the hanger bay.  **”If the defenses are tiered like the data is showing we’re going to have to leapfrog from point to point and deal with any defenses we come across.  Either taking it out or having Marina and Sera gain control and make the defenses work for us.  There's a potential for an EM field set up as some form of defense.   If so we’re going to have to be cautious as size and strength of the EM field is unknown.  We don't want to fry our only way off that bucket.”**

Kael nods, his gaze fixed on the schematic as Wes details the entry plan and potential hazards. The grim smile is gone, replaced by a look of focused calculation.

**"Copy that, Wes. Ventral bay, covered approach, tiered defense expectation... sounds like you've got a solid initial plan for the ground team,"** he says, his voice all business. **"If it's truly a tiered system, identifying those internal chokepoints where their 'silent guardians' are likely concentrated will be key for Marina and Sera before you push too deep."**

He taps his console, likely inputting a mental note. **"The EM field is a primary concern for the *Skimmer*. If your team's initial EVA sweep on boarding detects significant EM flux affecting your suit systems, or if our short-range sensors pick it up as we get closer, I'll hold station outside the main hangar structure. We can't risk frying the *Skimmer's* nav or primary controls. You'll have to make your way in from a tethered position or find an alternative entry point if the bay itself is the source."**

He glances at Wes. **"Assume full EVA conditions from the moment we drop out of hyperspace in the Shallows until your team confirms otherwise inside the *Shadow*. My suit recyclers are topped off, but yours will be on the clock once you're out. Make every excursion count."**

His focus returns to his instruments. **"My job remains the same: get you in as safely as possible, keep this bird ready to fly at a moment's notice, and be your exit strategy. You handle the ghosts and the salvage; I'll handle the void."**

**”We’ll make several scans of the *Shadow*  The fewer surprises the better.   If there’s any EM fields or fluxuations we’ll find them if they are there.   If EVA is necessary we’ll be ready.”**  He turned back to his station and sent the initial plan he had come up with along with Kael’s concerns about EM interference and the potential changes of landing in the hangar bay to an EVA breach if needed.   


**”I’ll send this initial plan to the rest of the team.   See what they think.   I’ll also add in your concerns about EM and make sure that’s one of the first things we scan for.”**   If an EVA was needed to breach the *Shadow* they were going to need a different plan.

Kael gives a sharp, single nod, his eyes still on the hyperspace display. **"Copy that. A solid plan. No arguments here."** He appreciates the clarity and the fact that Wes is taking his concerns seriously. **"First sign of heavy EM interference, I'll recommend we hold off and deploy you via EVA. I'll start running low-level diagnostics on the *Skimmer's* external sensors now, get a clean baseline before we drop into the Shallows. The less noise we have to filter, the faster we'll spot trouble."** He turns back to his console, his hands already moving, bringing up new data streams. The conversation, for him, is efficiently concluded.

The *Void Skimmer* continues its smooth, silent journey through the blue-white tunnel of hyperspace. In the main hold, the datapad of each team member chimes softly with the arrival of Wes's initial plan.

In their corner, Sera and Marina briefly pause their work. Marina reads the message, her expression thoughtful, giving a slow nod of approval at the emphasis on caution and the EVA contingency. It's a sensible approach that prioritizes both the mission and the safety of the team and their shuttle. She exchanges a look with Sera, who simply raises an eyebrow and offers a half-smile, a silent "makes sense" gesture, before they both turn back to the archaic schematics, their search now subtly reframed by the need to identify potential EVA-friendly entry points like maintenance hatches or damaged cargo doors.

Further aft, Roric and Echo receive the plan with practiced professionalism. Roric gives the message a quick read before leaning back in his acceleration couch, his eyes closing as he conserves energy, his mind likely processing the tactical shift from a hangar assault to a potential hull breach. Echo, ever vigilant, acknowledges the message on her bracer and then begins a meticulous, quiet check of the seals and oxygen levels on her own EVA-capable gear, preparing for the worst-case scenario as a matter of routine.

The team settles into a new rhythm of focused, quiet preparation, the reality of the dangerous task ahead now cemented by their leader's first official plan. Hours stretch before them in the humming quiet of hyperspace.

The monotonous hum of the hyperdrive abruptly cuts out, replaced by the jarring lurch of the *Void Skimmer* reverting to realspace. A chorus of proximity alarms screams to life for a split second before Kael slaps a control panel, silencing them. The view through the cockpit viewport is breathtaking and deeply unsettling.

They have arrived in the Graveyard Shallows.

It is not merely an asteroid field; it is a tomb. The skeletal frames of ancient, shattered starships, some kilometers long, drift in a slow, silent funeral procession. Twisted metal spires, remnants of long-dead vessels, float like forgotten tombstones amidst a dense field of scarred asteroids and icy debris. The entire scene is bathed in the eerie, ghostly light of the distant Lesser Karthon Nebula, casting long, distorted shadows that make it impossible to judge distance or scale accurately. The ship's comms crackle with a constant, low static hiss.

**"Kriff... look at this mess,"** Kael breathes, his voice tight with focus. He's no longer leaning back; he's wrestling with the controls, his hands a blur as the *Void Skimmer* is buffeted by unseen gravitic tides. **"Sensors are a nightmare. I've got ghost signals and energy spikes popping up all over the scope. Getting a clean lock on anything is going to be hell."**

Looming in the center of the chaotic field, partially embedded in the pitted surface of a massive, rotating asteroid, is their target: the *Veiled Shadow*. It is a mountain of dark, dead metal, its design archaic and alien. Its hull is pocked with countless micro-craters and a few larger impact scars, but shows no obvious signs of catastrophic damage, nor any signs of life or power. It drifts silently, a colossal ghost ship waiting in its grave.

Through the Force, Wes can feel the profound, oppressive silence of the place. It's an ancient cold, a sense of deep dormancy and millennia of waiting that seems to emanate from the derelict. The immediate challenge is clear: navigating the treacherous final few kilometers to the *Veiled Shadow* without being torn apart by the graveyard's unseen hazards.

After sending out the his initial plan with the possible chance of an EVA entry into the *Shadow* Wes set about going over his armor and systems.  Making sure all seals were reading green on his HUD.  Oxygen levels were topped off as was fuel for his jet pack.   When he was satisfied that his armor and weapons were all set he looked over the co-pilots station.  Seeing that nothing needed his immediate attention he sat back in the chair and got as comfortable as possible.    


Being Mandalorian came with a learned ability to find sleep whenever possible.  One never knew when they would actually get some rack time so the practice of sleeping whenever and wherever was ingrained in them all.   Now he also had the ability to enter a light Force trance.   He was still sleeping but also of his surroundings.   It allowed for a more restful sleep in a short amount of time.   **”Kael I’m going to take some time to rest while I can.”**  With that he closed his eyes.   Reaching out with his senses he felt the Force around him and used it to enter a light sleep.  The low rhythmic thrum of the hyperdrive further helped him relax.   


The sudden drop out of hyperspace coupled with the shudder of then *Skimmer* and proximity alarms had Wes awake immediately as his hands went out to his station's controls as he was jolted around in his chair.    Listening to Kael. Wes nodded even though the point could see his acknowledgement.   **”Copy.   I’ll see if I can clean up the scope some.   You just keep us from being the Mynock on the canopy.”**  Wes moved a hand to the sensor controls to try and clean things up.

Wes’s hands move to the co-pilot's sensor controls, his fingers dancing across the archaic interface with a surprising, instinctive familiarity. Drawing on his early training with similar GAT-12 systems and augmenting it with a nascent Force-tuned intuition, he doesn’t just adjust the gain; he starts actively recalibrating the sensor frequencies, creating cascading filters to isolate the ship's own energy signature and cancel out the nebula's distorting feedback.

The chaotic mess on the main sensor display, once a snowstorm of ghost signals and false readings, begins to resolve with stunning clarity. The phantom contacts evaporate, leaving behind only the hard, clear signals of the real threats: the precise locations of tumbling asteroids, the shifting vectors of larger derelict fragments, and the shimmering outlines of the gravitic eddies Kael was fighting to avoid. A clear, if narrow, path towards the *Veiled Shadow* now materializes on the tactical display.

Kael lets out a low whistle, his knuckles no longer white on the control yoke. **"Kriff, Wes... where'd a Mandalorian learn to tune an antique CEC sensor suite like that? You just took the guesswork out of this whole approach."** His relief is palpable as he eases the *Void Skimmer* into a smoother, more deliberate course through the now-charted hazards. **"Path is clearing up. I can see the primary hazards now. Bringing us in slow and steady."**

The lurching of the *Void Skimmer* smooths out into a stable flight path as Kael confidently navigates the now-defined route. The colossal, silent shape of the *Veiled Shadow*, embedded in its asteroid shroud, grows steadily larger in the main viewport, dominating the view. The immediate danger of the graveyard has been expertly managed, replaced by the looming, ancient mystery of their destination.

As his fingers worked across the older sensor systems he fell into a rhythm.  He remembered the instructions from his parents.  His mother always had a way with the older sensor systems and she made sure to try and pass on the knowledge.   He adjusted the sensor frequencies up and down as needed to pick out, isolate and remove what wasn’t a problem for them.  As the false readings and sensor ghosts dwindled he heard Kael beside him as a small yet narrow path emerged before them.   **”My mother.”**  He smiled as he answered the pilot.   Despite the smile there was a hit of sadness as he remembered her and the void her passing left.  Surprised by the sudden emotion he attempted to compartmentalize the feelings for later.  There was slight hitch in his movements but there was no one to notice as Kael was focused on keeping the *Skimmer* on the path he just paid out.   


**”Slow and steady.   Copy that Kael.  Take us in.”**  He kept an eye on the sensors as Kael began the thread the needle towards the *Veiled Shadow*.  After making one final adjustment he allowed himself to look out at the view of their approach.  He took in the size of their target that was nestled hidden and secure in its location.   He put his focus on their intended target.  The *Shadow* could be seen clearly as it drifted in the chaos.  Opening up his senses he reached out to the derelict with the Force and along their path.

The *Void Skimmer* glides through the last of the major debris, Kael's piloting now sure and steady thanks to the clean sensor data. The *Veiled Shadow* looms before them, a breathtaking sight. It is truly immense, a dark leviathan of a ship, its hull plating a patchwork of ancient impacts and cosmic erosion. Its design is unlike anything Wes has seen before, with sweeping, almost organic lines that contrast with its colossal scale.

Wes extends his senses, reaching out with the Force as they close the distance. He feels that same profound, ancient silence emanating from the derelict, a deep dormancy that feels less like a dead ship and more like a slumbering beast. There are no signs of active life, no whispers of conscious thought. However, he does feel something else – a subtle *wrongness* in the space around a specific section of the ship's port side. It's not a direct threat, but a faint, dissonant note in the Force, like a distorted echo or a place where the natural flow of energy is being subtly warped. It seems to emanate from a section of the hull that appears unusually dark, almost as if it's absorbing the faint light of the nebula.

Simultaneously, his sharp eyes, aided by the cockpit's enhanced viewport, catch a detail that confirms his Force sense. The section of the port hull he was sensing isn't just dark; it appears to have a different texture. While most of the ship is scarred and pitted, this large patch is unnervingly smooth, almost featureless. It doesn't reflect light the same way as the surrounding metal. More importantly, several smaller pieces of debris that drift close to this area seem to subtly alter their trajectory, nudged away by an invisible force before making contact. It's not a powerful shield, but some kind of low-energy, passive defensive field is clearly still active there.

Kael, focused on his approach to the massive ventral hangar bay, hadn't noticed the subtle anomaly. **"Almost there,"** he reports, his voice calm. **"Hangar bay doors appear sealed, but there are no obvious signs of catastrophic damage around the frame. Looks like our primary entry point is viable, pending a closer scan."**

Wes looked at the derelict hulk of the *Veiled Shadow*.   It was bigger than he thought which meant it could take longer to navigate to find what they needed.  It also meant that the longer they were there the more dangerous this dead hulk of a ship became.  


Yet the ship felt silent, not dead.  As if something was dormant waiting for a reason to awaken.  There was no life as he understood aboard but there was something out of place.   Something that didn’t belong.   Another moment of searching the hull he spotted it. A small section on the port side had a presence in the force.  It felt distorted to him with his limited knowledge and experience.   It was more of a gut feeling that it was wrong.  That feeling and the fact that the section seemed darker than the surrounding area casted an eerie feeling beyond the visual sense of light seeming to not escape it.   


**”Something feels off.  Maintain this course but avoid this section for now.  Looks like the landing bay may be an option.”**  He said as he tapped on his controls and brought up an enhanced image of the distorted section for Kael to see.   They both watched as debris seemed to deflect off the area.  Dialing on the sensors he looked at it more closely.   **Low power energy shield maybe.  I don’t know.   I’ll see if Marina and Sera can get a read on it.”**  He said as he opened up the ships comms and sent the images of the ship and the shielded spot that felt off to him to the two the two ladies he was leaning on for tech support.    


**”Marina , Sera.  I’m sending you a pic to scan.   Take a look and report back.   Marina, reach out to it, tell me what you feel.  That space feels off for lack of a better word.”**

Wes’s message, along with the enhanced sensor image of the dark, smooth patch of hull, appears on a shared terminal screen in the *Void Skimmer's* hold. Marina and Sera lean closer, their expressions shifting from general readiness to intense, professional focus. Marina’s fingers dance across the controls, cross-referencing the live feed with the archaic schematics she pulled from the archives, while Sera tethers her own datapad, running diagnostic filters on the signal data Wes is feeding them.

After a moment of shared, silent analysis, Marina’s voice comes over the internal comms, sharp with intrigue.

**"Wes, we see it. Your 'off' feeling corresponds with the sensor data. That section isn't just dark; it's actively absorbing our passive sensor pings. The energy field deflecting the debris is low-power, more like a minimal repulsor screen than a combat shield, but the combination is... highly unusual. It could be some kind of archaic energy-absorbent plating, or..."** She trails off for a second, considering a more audacious possibility. **"...a localized stealth field of some kind, ancient and malfunctioning."**

Sera looks up from her datapad, her eyes locking onto the image. **"If it's a stealth field, even a broken one, its power source could be unstable,"** she adds, her tone now all business. **"More importantly, it creates a tactical blind spot. We have no idea what's behind that patch of hull, what condition it's in, or if there are weapon emplacements concealed by it. We have to assume that entire section is a major threat until we can get a direct visual or find a way to bypass that field from an internal system."**

Wes looked at their target as he listened to them talk though ideas.   He felt he needed them to understand.  **”Marina is not just a gut feeling, I can feel it in the Force.  It feels *wrong*.   Not in a direct threat but caution is warranted.  Whatever it is or whoever caused it, it's been there for a long time.”**   He adjusted his scanners.   **”First priority is a safe landing spot.   Let’s find one.”**

Wes focuses on the sensor console, his fingers moving with a newfound purpose. He cross-references the initial scans of the massive ventral hangar bay with the "Nomad Class" schematics Marina provided, looking for structural patterns that might indicate a safer, more defensible position. After a few tense moments of filtering through the static and ghost signals, he isolates a promising location on the display. It's a recessed docking bay or maintenance alcove set into the port side wall of the main hangar, large enough for the *Void Skimmer* but offering significant cover from the wide-open central expanse. Initial structural integrity scans of that specific section appear stable.

As Kael flew the *Skimmer* with expert ease, Wes was busy at the sensor controls.  They needed a somewhat defensible position to touch down.   In the middle of the hanger bay, if it was like what they thought, was a no go.  He looked for what they had talked about earlier.   Something off to the side with an access way close by.   Several minutes passed along with a few curse words as he fought with the static and ghost signals.   Finally with a minute adjustment the screen cleared and he had the image he was looking for.    


**”There.”**  He pointed to a spot on the screen.  As he took an image capture and marked it up with a possible landing point.   He also pointed out what looked like probably egress and ingress points from their possible landing point. He sent off the image to the others.  **”Marina, see if you and Sera can plot out plausible routes to where we need to go and correlate that with possible terminal access points and defenses.   Roric, Echo work them to point out possible ambush and pincer points for the ships defenses and or any other interlopers that may also be there.”**

Kael leans forward, studying the marked-up schematic Wes sends to the main pilot's console. He cross-references it with his own sensor readings. **"Copy that, Wes. I see the alcove. Looks stable from here. A good defensive position."** His hands move with practiced ease over the controls, adjusting the *Void Skimmer's* trajectory. **"Taking us in. Switching to maneuvering thrusters. It's gonna be quiet out there, so expect the landing struts to sound loud."**

The main engines fall to a low hum as Kael expertly guides the blastboat towards the colossal opening of the ventral hangar bay. The interior of the derelict is a breathtaking vista of silent, ancient grandeur. Dust motes, undisturbed for millennia, dance in the beams of the *Skimmer's* powerful external lights, illuminating a cavernous space that dwarfs their small shuttle. Massive, silent gantries and what might have been docking arms hang like skeletal remains from the distant ceiling. The scale is oppressive, humbling.

Kael navigates the shuttle smoothly across the vast expanse, heading directly for the recessed maintenance bay Wes had identified. With a final, gentle burst from the thrusters, the *Void Skimmer* settles onto the deck plating with a series of loud, echoing **THUDS** that reverberate through the silent hangar. The engines whine down to a low idle, and a profound silence descends, broken only by the low hum of their own ship's life support.

In the main hold, Marina receives the marked-up schematic. **"Got it. Plotting potential routes from this position to the primary engineering and data core locations based on the Void-Barge layout."** She pulls the image onto the shared display, her datapad already glowing with highlighted pathways and potential obstacles.

Roric Jannsen, having received the plan, is now fully encased in his armor, his helmet's seals engaged with a soft hiss. He moves to one of the small portholes in the hold, peering out into the dusty gloom of the hangar. **"Clear landing zone. Good cover from the main bay,"** he grunts, his voice slightly filtered by his helmet's internal comm. **"Echo, on me. Let's review potential entry and exit points from the ramp. We establish a perimeter the second that hatch opens."**

Echo is already there, her own sleek helmet sealed, the tinted visor reflecting the dim light of the hold. She gives a brief nod. **"Agreed,"** she says, her voice equally crisp over the comms. **"I'll take point on initial sweep, check for atmospheric integrity and immediate threats within a twenty-meter arc. You cover the ramp. We leapfrog from there."**
The team is a coil of potential energy, their initial plan in motion, waiting in the eerie silence of the ghost ship's hangar for their leader's command to begin.

Wes listened as Kael talked about the chosen spot to set down the *Void Skimmer.*  **”Good.  Get us into position and keep her hot.  You’re our ticket out of here.  Monitor comms and keep an eye out.  Let us know if *anything*  changes.  Good work Kael.”**  He finished with a nod as he stood.  With a final tap he put his station on standby.  All it needed was the hit of a button to spring back into action.  


In his helmet he could hear the comms of the rest of the team.  Marina and Sera were prepping a way to get the mission critical items as Roric and Echo set the plan to establish their secure ‘beachhead’ in the massive hanger bay.   He defended the ladder down to the common area where the rest of his team was ready.   Wes reached for the second Ion rifle and gave it a quick look over before his gaze fell to his team.   He gave them all a nod as he activated his armor’s seals and felt the oxygen start to run as well as the ten hour timer for his oxygen supply.  The timer ticked off in the upper right corner of his HUD.    


**”Let’s go to work.”**

**"Copy that, Wes. Keeping her warm,"** Kael's says to Wes as the young Mandalorian places his consoleon standby and departs, his attention already fully on the *Skimmer's* systems and the silent, imposing view outside the cockpit.

Marina and Sera wrap up their initial data overlay, sharing the compiled routes and potential threat markers to the rest of the team's datapads. **"Okay, preliminary paths are set,"** Marina confirms to Wes over the comm. **"Sending them to your HUD now."** They quickly secure their equipment, then move to a designated storage locker in the main hold. Inside are four standard-issue shipboard EVA suits, essential for operating in the unknown environment of the *Veiled Shadow*.

Sera grabs a suit and begins donning it with a practiced, efficient series of movements that suggest she's done this many times before. She snaps the seals on the boots and gloves, her motions fluid and economical.
