Wes couldn’t believe his eyes.  Then again they were on a millennia old ship in the middle of nowhere so why couldn’t there be another ship here.   It was another mystery that perhaps may not be solved.  Getting on the ship would prove useful in finding out more.  


**”Alright.  Marina, check that auxiliary cargo hatch.  Echo, give her a hand with that manual release.    Roric, maintain cover on our entry point.   I doubt we will have any surprises but better safe than sorry.”**   He looked at Sera who was  tapping away on her data pad that was still hooked in.   **”Anything else Sera?   If not, when we get access to the ship, find a terminal and try there.   Let’s see if this ship's crew did any salvaging when they were here.   Look for any records of crew logs and possible salvage.  Let’s find out who they were, where they were from and who they were affiliated with.   Maybe they already did some of our work for us.  And their cargo hold is full of what we need.”**


Wes followed Marina and Echo to the auxiliary cargo hatch all the while keeping himself open to the Force trusting it to guide him here in the cargo bay.
**"Copy, Wes. On it,"** Echo confirms, moving to the auxiliary cargo hatch alongside Marina.
**"Holding rear security. Nothing to report,"** Roric's voice replies, a steady presence at their backs.
From her terminal, Sera adds, **"Acknowledged. I'll keep digging in these hangar logs while you work on the door. If I find a crew manifest, I'll send it over."**

Marina runs her gloved hand over the seized manual release, her suit light illuminating the heavy, dust-caked lever. **"The mechanism is frozen solid with age,"** she reports. **"I can apply a solvent to the main joint, but I'll need some brute force to break the initial seal. Echo, if you can put your weight here..."** She points to a specific point on the lever's housing.

Echo holsters her carbine and plants her feet, her agile frame tensing as she grips the lever. Working together, Marina applies the solvent while Echo puts her full weight into a series of sharp, controlled shoves. For a long moment, nothing happens. Then, with a jarring **CRACK** that vibrates through their boots, the ancient mechanism gives way. The auxiliary cargo hatch groans open a few feet before stopping, its movement kicking up a silent cloud of dust into the vacuum of the hangar bay.

Without hesitation, Echo slips through the opening, her tactical light cutting a sharp, clean path through the darkness inside. A few tense seconds of silence pass. **"Interior is clear,"** she reports, her voice crisp over the comm. **"Looks like a standard Ghtroc 720 cargo hold. Everything is powered down. Thick layer of dust. My suit's external sensors are registering a thin, non-breathable atmosphere inside—mostly inert gases. It's venting into the hangar now. No signs of immediate hazard."**

Marina follows her in, her own light playing over the silent hold. **"Looks like it's been untouched for decades,"** she murmurs, her voice a mix of awe and professional curiosity. **"No signs of a struggle. It's... just empty."**

The interior of the *Seventh Moon* is a time capsule. The cargo hold is mostly empty, save for a few secured but vacant cargo containers. A fine layer of dust coats every surface. The thin, stale atmosphere that had been trapped inside for decades quickly vents into the vacuum of the hangar, leaving the ship's interior as silent and airless as the bay outside. The only light comes from their suits, revealing a dark, silent path forward into the main corridor of the Ghtroc freighter.

**”Echo, finish sweeping the aft section of the ship.  I’ll head fore with Marina and Sera to the cockpit.  Should be able to access more of the ships logs from there, find out why they were here and maybe what happened to them.”**  He gestured to the access way that led to the cockpit.  **”Let's go.”**  As he turned to lead the way he spoke to Roric.   **”Ror if things look good there take a look around the cargo bay see if there’s anything else worth noting .   If we’re taking the time to check this out let’s do it right and look at all we can.”**


**”Any ideas on why someone else would have come out here?”**

**"Copy, Wes. Sweeping the aft sections now,"** Echo confirms. She turns, her light probing the engine room access and the crew quarter corridors at the rear of the Ghtroc, moving with silent efficiency.

**"Understood,"** Roric grunts in reply. **"Securing the entry point and beginning a full sweep of this hangar bay."** He steps away from the *Seventh Moon*, his own suit light beginning a methodical scan of the surrounding containers and the dusty deck of the *Veiled Shadow's* hidden bay.

Marina nods, her own light following Wes's gesture towards the forward accessway. **"As for why they came out here... the Pathfinders came looking for Force vergences or lost knowledge. But a private survey crew in a Ghtroc?"** She falls into step beside Wes as they move into the freighter's dark main corridor. **"Profit, most likely. The Karthon Expanse is treacherous, but it's also filled with wrecks. Some spacers believe ancient derelicts like the *Shadow* hold priceless artifacts, lost technology, or even caches of valuable rare metals. It's a high-risk, high-reward gamble for a small, independent crew."**

Sera follows closely behind them, her datapad already active, scanning for any network traces. **"She's right,"** Sera adds. **"Or they were looking for something specific. That log entry said 'long-term survey preparation'. That's not a quick smash-and-grab salvage run. That's a planned expedition. They were either very dedicated, or they knew something was here that was worth a long, dangerous stay."**

The trio makes their way through the silent, dust-filled corridors of the *Seventh Moon*. The layout is familiar to anyone who's been on a light freighter, but the decades of neglect give it an eerie, tomb-like quality. They pass a small, spartan crew quarters and a tiny, dark galley before reaching the cockpit. The blast door is open. Inside, the cockpit is dark, the viewports caked with a thick layer of external grime. Everything is coated in the same fine dust, and the two pilot's chairs sit empty, silent witnesses to a long-forgotten final flight. A single, ancient-looking datapad rests on the co-pilot's console, seemingly left in a hurry.

As Wes and Sera step into the cockpit to examine the logs and the abandoned datapad, Marina pauses at the doorway. The cockpit is a dead end for her skills without power. Her gaze drifts back down the corridor, towards the aft of the ship where the real machinery lies.

**"Wes, Sera, you handle everything up here,"** she says over the comm, her voice already filled with a renewed sense of purpose. **"I'm going to head back to the engine room. If I can get a look at the power core and the primary conduits, I might be able to figure out why she's dead in the water. Maybe... just maybe... there's a way to coax some life back into her auxiliary power with a portable starter. We'll need lights and system access if we're going to do a thorough search."**

Without waiting for a reply, she turns and heads purposefully back down the silent corridor, her suit light a focused beam cutting through the darkness, disappearing towards the rear of the freighter. She moves with the confidence of a mechanic heading into her natural habitat, the mystery of a dead engine a far more tangible and engaging puzzle for her than cryptic logs or dusty datapads.

The walk through the freighter had both Sera and Marina giving their thoughts on why the freighter was here.  Both had valid points from different points of view.  Either way it was their fortune that the ship was here.  **”Well whoever they were they did go through considerable lengths to set up a point of entry.   Almost positive all this  wasn’t done in one go around.   They must have made several trips.”**


Stepping into the cockpit Wes looked around.   The inside like everything else was covered with the fine dust that was prevalent everywhere they had been so far.  The viewports had even more caked on dust and grime.    


When Marina mentioned heading aft he turned and gave her a nod.  **”Copy, have Echo check in with you when she’s done her security sweep.   An extra pair of hands won’t hurt.”**  He sent along the feeling of confidence to her.  **”We’ll handle this, check in when you’ve dialed in on the situation back there.”**  He returned the small smile she gave him before turning away to proceed back the way they had come.   


Turning back to the cockpit he moved forward.   Looking over the two empty pilot chairs and the surrounding cramped area he reached out with his senses.  The immediate area around them was quiet in the Force.  There was himself and Sera who had her own unique signature.  **”We won’t get much until we have some power to light things up.  Can you supply power to the datapad?   Get it up and running while we wait for Marina and Echo.”**  He reached for the datapad and handed it over to Sera before taking a seat in one of the chairs.

Sera takes the ancient datapad from Wes, her gloved fingers expertly brushing away the thickest layer of dust. **"Let's see what secrets you're keeping, old timer,"** she mutters. She pulls a specialized power adapter and a thin interface cable from her slicing kit. **"The power port is an older standard, but I've got a match. Supplying minimal power from my portable pack... now."**

The datapad's screen flickers hesitantly, then glows to life with a weak, greenish light, displaying an archaic, text-based operating system. A few moments of Sera's focused tapping follows, bypassing a simple, time-degraded security lockout.

**"Okay, I'm in,"** she reports, her eyes scanning the screen. **"The good news is, the core memory is intact. The bad news... the data is heavily fragmented, and the processor on this thing is a fossil. I'm seeing multiple corrupted files, partial crew logs, and what looks like a very complex astrogation chart, but it's all jumbled."**

She sighs in frustration. **"I can try to run a basic file recovery sequence, but it's going to be incredibly slow. To do a deep-level data reconstruction and actually make sense of these fragments, I'd need to slave this thing to a much more powerful system... like the *Void Skimmer's* main computer."**

She looks up at Wes, a practical frown on her face. **"I might be able to pull a few clear text files or a crew manifest if we're lucky, but it'll take time. Getting the full picture—the 'why' and 'what happened'—isn't happening on this thing alone."**

He was looking over the ship's controls as Sera looked over the datapad.  The cockpit was in good working order aside from being shut down for some odd 70 plus years.   When Sera looked up he saw a frown on her face at not having the tools on hand to really dig into the treasure of information that she held in her hands.  He could feel her want to crack the defragmentation and he smiled.  He understood what it felt like to want something.   His gaze lifted up from Sera’s lit helmet to the corridor that Marina had walked down to head aft.  He wondered how her efforts were progressing.  

**”Do what you can.  It’s not a priority right now.  Back burner it when Marina get us some auxiliary power.   Maybe the ship's computer core will be in better shape to pry some info from.”**  He brought his gaze back from the corridor  back to Sera.   He knew that Zeltrons had an empathic ability and then she could probably pick up on his emotions similarly like he could.   Mostly like better at it than he was.  Still they were teammates and they were waiting.  **”How did you get wrapped up with the Altisan Jedi?”**  He asked unsure if this was a topic she’d be willing to talk about.

Sera nods, setting the ancient datapad carefully to one side on the console. **"Copy that, Wes. I'll run a slow background recovery, see if anything simple shakes loose."** She appreciates the professional trust and the clear direction.

When he asks about her past, her focused, professional demeanor softens. The question is personal, but his tone is open and genuine. She leans back slightly in the co-pilot's chair she's claimed, the green glow of the datapad's screen illuminating her crimson features.

**"Wrapped up with them?"** she repeats, a small, wry smile touching her lips. **"That's a good way to put it. It wasn't exactly a planned career move."** She idly traces a pattern on the dusty console. **"My family... we were traders. Independent haulers, mostly working the Outer Rim. We weren't smugglers, not really, but we weren't picky about our cargo, and we knew how to stay off the *Republic's*... more annoying shipping manifests."**

Her expression becomes a little more somber. **"Things got... complicated, a couple of years ago. A deal with a local syndicate went bad. We weren't trying to cheat them, but our client did, and the syndicate didn't care about the details. They came after our ship, our cargo... us."** She looks away for a moment, her gaze distant. **"We were cornered in a failing orbit over a backwater moon, no fuel, no allies, and a Hutt's corvette getting ready to blast us into scrap. We thought that was it."**

She looks back at Wes, her eyes holding a deep, sincere gratitude. **"And then... a battered old Consular-class cruiser dropped out of hyperspace. Altisian markings. They answered a distress call no one else would touch. Master Lissandra Sey was on board... she mediated. Talked the syndicate down, offered them something that was apparently more valuable than simple revenge. I don't know the full details, but she saved us. All of us."**

**"After that,"** she finishes with a slight shrug, **"sticking with my family's 'ethically flexible' trading business didn't feel right anymore. The Altisians offered us sanctuary aboard the *Chu'unthor*. My family eventually moved on to a neutral system, but I stayed. Figured the least I could do was offer my... particular talents... to the people who saved our lives. The galaxy's a dangerous place. It helps to have someone watching your back who actually cares about more than just the next paycheck."**

Wes listened as she started talking. He sensed that she was keeping it close at first but the more she talked the more she opened up.  It wasn’t just her story, it was her family.  A story about a family business who for the most part ran things above board.  Wes knew, like many, sometimes above board wasn’t  always possible.  Especially in Sera’s family's case, when the client is working on good faith.  


Her story had taken a turn but was corrected by the faithful intervention of the Altisan Jedi.   He smiled a little when she talked about staying behind as he family moved on to a neutral planet.  Walking a different path than one’s own family wasn’t easy.   It meant that Sera had a strong sense of purpose to do what she felt was right.   It was something he was familiar with.   He also had a choice.   Stay with the Clans or pursue a vastly different path.  He thought about all that had happened.  Fakir.  Meeting Marina, falling in love with her and their still growing bond.  Discovering his affinity with the Force and their growing baby within her.   His gaze shifted towards the rear of the ship to where she worked.  He could sense and feel her emotions as easily as breathing.  She was happy right now putting her skills to good use.  He smiled as he looked back at Sera knowing full well that she was able to sense most of what he was feeling.  


**”Sometimes the plan we had for ourselves finds a different path and or meaning.  If I had been told that I’d be here right now doing what I’m doing I would have said they were crazy for such a grandiose fantasy.    Yet here I am.  I am learning that we all make our choices but sometimes the Force gives us a nudge and it’s up to us to either listen or not.”**  He gave her a small smile thankful that she shared something about herself.  **”I think we all have made that choice to listen otherwise we wouldn’t be here right now.”**

Sera nods slowly, her gaze following Wes's for a moment as he looks back down the corridor where Marina had gone. She can't feel the precise connection he shares with Marina, that deep, resonant hum in the Force, but even without it, her natural Zeltron empathy allows her to sense the powerful emotions radiating from him: the profound affection, the fierce protectiveness, the quiet joy that underpins his very presence when he thinks of her. It's a current far stronger than the simple camaraderie of teammates.

She brings her perceptive eyes back to him, a soft, knowing smile on her face. **"A nudge from the Force,"** she repeats his words, her tone thoughtful. **"Yeah, I suppose you could call it that. Or maybe just seeing people willing to put themselves on the line for strangers reminds you that there are things worth fighting for beyond just your own survival."**

She leans back in her seat, the professional slicer momentarily giving way to the young woman who found a new home. **"I'm glad I chose to listen, too, Wes. It's... good to be part of something that's trying to build, even when everything else seems to be burning down."** Her smile becomes a bit more genuine, a shared understanding passing between them. **"And it seems like you and Marina found something pretty special to build together, too."**

As Sera explained that she wanted to help others.  Seeing the Altisan Jedi and what they did lit a fire under her to do the same.   He nodded in agreement. Happy to know that others also felt the same way.   He watched as she leaned back in the seat as she became more comfortable.  The slicer personality set onto the side slightly.   The smile she gave him was a comfortable one and her following question about his relationship with Marina wasn’t surprising.  Her empathetic abilities were apparently on point.  


He gave her a small half smile as if he’d been found out.  He gave her a nod.  **”Special is a very good way to put it.  Neither of us were looking for it but found us and when our guards were let down it took hold.”**  He gave Sera a small smile knowing that she sensed the raw emotion coming from him.  **”Marina and I have a special connection.  It was one that the main order didn’t like.  What’s important is that we found our way back to each other.   We have much to look forward to.”**

Sera's smile softens, becoming less playful and more genuinely warm. She follows his gaze for a moment as he looks back towards the corridor where Marina is working. Her own Zeltron empathy easily picks up on the complex wave of emotions coming from him – not just affection, but a fierce, profound protectiveness and a powerful, forward-looking hope.

**"It's good that you found your way back to each other,"** she says softly. **"The kind of hope you're putting out right now... it's a powerful thing. It's more than just about two people finding each other."**

She meets his eyes, her gaze knowing but kind. **"It's the kind of hope that's about building something new. Something to protect. Something that lasts."** She gives a small, supportive nod, not pressing the issue further. **"That's a good thing to have to fight for, Wes. Especially now."**

The Mandalorian's smile remained as Sera spoke.  That she could see how much Marina meant to him almost made him blush.  Thankfully his helmet hid such things.  Yet he knew her empathic sense picked up on his emotions.  


What she said about building something new made a lot of sense.  After Order 66 the new Galactic Empire had set about to wipe out the Jedi Order and anyone who supported it.   Surely the Emperor knew that any remaining Jedi, even just a few, would eventually come back and cause problems for the fledgling Empire.   Perhaps ending its reign of terror.   


**”We all know that the Republic wasn’t perfect but it was light years better than the subjugation the Emperor is throwing down all over the Galaxy.  We bide our time. Grow our strengths and see what happens.   Maybe someday there will be a resistance.   If and when there is, many will follow, myself included.  Can't let what we’re fighting for be in vain.”**

Sera’s smile fades, replaced by a look of serious, thoughtful agreement. Her own empathy resonates with the fierce resolve she feels coming from him, a conviction that goes beyond the immediate mission. She leans forward slightly, the glow from the consoles casting shifting shadows on her crimson features.

**"Heh. 'Not perfect' is a polite way to put it,"** she says, her voice a little quieter now, more intense. **"My family... we spent our whole lives in the spaces the Republic Senators on Coruscant forgot existed. We saw plenty of corruption, plenty of greed."** She shakes her head. **"But at least there were spaces to *be* forgotten in. The Empire... from what I've seen, they don't seem like the type to leave any corner of the galaxy unturned or any life uncatalogued."**

**"So yeah, Wes,"** she concludes, her voice firm with commitment. **"When that day comes... you can count me in. What we're doing here, starting now... it's a part of that fight. It has to be."**

The quiet, focused atmosphere in the Ghtroc's cockpit is suddenly broken by the crackle of the internal comm. It's Marina, and the background of her transmission is not the silence of the corridor, but a low, resonant hum of machinery and the faint, rhythmic clank of a piston.

**"Wes, Sera? It's Marina. A few tense seconds of silence pass. Then, a deep, powerful **WHOOMPH** vibrates through the entire Ghtroc freighter, a sound felt as much as heard. **"We have auxiliary power! It's stable! The old girl is waking up!"**

We’re just a small part.  It will be the galaxy that needs to wake up and take charge.  I just hope that the Council back on the Chu’unthor sees that as well.”**  He gave Sera a nod as a feeling of thankfulness came forward.   **”Thank you Sera. No sooner had he finished speaking Marina’s voice came crackling across the comms.  Wes could tell by her voice she was excited by what she had found in engineering.   With a quick run down from her he was turning towards the Ghtroc’s controls.   **”Light her up Marina.  Keep us informed.”** 


Moments later Wes felt the telltale shudder followed by the vibration of the entire ship felt through their feet and where they sat in the pilots chairs.   His hands were already looking over the status boards of the freighter making sure things came up in the green.  When Marina’s voice returned her jubilation was evident in her voice.  **”Copy that Marina.  Good work you two.  Already looking over things up here.  So far so good.  Sera,”**  He paused long enough to look over to her.  **”See if there may be any more ship personnel and cargo logs or even copies of what’s on that datapad.   Maybe the ship's main computer core, even as outdated as it is, may be in better shape than the pad.”**


He looked down to the controls as more lights came to life.    He was beginning to wonder if this old bucket would fly.  He activated the team comms once more.   **”Roric, how's the cargo bay looking?   Anything we should take note of?”**

Sera grins, the cockpit lights reflecting in her crimson eyes as the Ghtroc's systems come to life around them. **"You got it, Wes. If this ship's main computer is online, it's a thousand times more powerful than that handheld relic. I'll tap into the central data core and see what I can pull. Roric's gruff voice crackles over the comms from his position back in the *Veiled Shadow's* cavernous hangar bay. **"All quiet out here, Wes. The Ghtroc powering up didn't seem to stir anything. Hangar is still dark, still silent. Your call on whether that's a good thing or a bad thing."**

Wes gave Sera a nod.. She didn’t see it as she had already dove into the task he gave her.  With a half smile he looked over the power readings.  Everything was coming up green and that was good.   It meant that the ship had a stable core which was surprising.   Roric’s statement had him thinking.   How would an active power source show up to the ship's ‘consciousness’?  **”I’ll get back to you on that Ror.   You just made me think of something.  Marina, Sera, could firing up the ships power core register as anything bad to the *Veiled Shadows consciousness?”**  


Calmly he reached out with his senses pushing past the walls of the ‘secret’ hanger bay to where the giant maintenance droids were lying dormant.  If firing up the ship set off alarms they were going to be having company.

Sera, her attention snapping from the Ghtroc's newly awakened console to the data she's still pulling from the *Veiled Shadow*, frowns in concentration. **"That's the million-credit question, isn't it?"** she says over the comm, her voice a low, focused murmur. **"On a modern security grid, activating an unknown power signature inside a sealed, unlisted bay would have alarms screaming and security teams kicking down the door. It's possible this bay is so well isolated that the mothership's main consciousness doesn't even know we're here. Or, it's a local alert, and we just woke up the neighborhood watch."**

Marina's voice cuts in from the Ghtroc's engine room, her tone thoughtful and laced with caution. **"Sera's right about the isolation, but this whole section of the ship resonates with the energy from that crystal, Wes. Introducing a new, active power source into that environment... it's like lighting a flare in a pitch-black, silent cave. It might not be seen as an immediate *threat*, but we have to assume it's been *noticed*. Any automated systems nearby are likely aware that *something* is now active in this section."**

As his teammates give their assessments, Wes extends his senses, pushing his Force awareness past the hull of the Ghtroc and back into the vast, silent corridors of the *Veiled Shadow*. He seeks out the cold voids he'd identified earlier—the three droids by the crystal and the two hidden in the flanking containers.

They are still there. Still dormant. But something has changed.

Before, they were utterly cold, inert spots in the Force. Now, they feel... poised. There's a faint, almost imperceptible trickle of energy flowing towards them, a subtle shift in their state from "off" to "standby." The threat is not immediate, the droids have not activated, but the potential for them to do so feels terrifyingly closer. The ship has indeed noticed them. It's waiting.

As he reached out with his senses he listened to Sera and Marina’s thoughts on the ship coming back to life.  Both were cautious as it seemed that none of them thought about the possible ramifications of firing up the old ship.   He continued to push further out and as he reached the points where the five droids were asleep in the vicinity of the crystal.   


His eyes widened and Marina, though their bond felt his caution spike.  They weren’t active but they had changed subtlety.    They were not active but he had that feeling that they could be at any moment.   He kept his senses focused on them as he spoke.   **”Hold increasing power gains.”**  There was a hint of urgency in his voice.   **”If anything, back them off.  Keep power to the main computer but stop increasing it anywhere else.”**  He paused to take a quick breath.   **”The power surge in here has been noticed by our large droid *friends.*  They aren’t actively moving but they now instead of being in and *off* mode they are on *standby* for lack of better wording.   We’re walking a fine line here.”**

**"Copy, Wes. Halting power-up sequence,"** Marina's voice comes back instantly from the engine room, her tone now tight with concern. **"Bringing auxiliary power back down to minimal levels, just enough to keep the computer core and local life support active. In the cockpit, Sera's hands fly across the console, terminating the deeper system scans she had initiated. **"Acknowledged. Isolating the main computer core from non-essential systems. Reducing my data probes to passive monitoring only,"** she reports, her earlier excitement replaced by a focused, professional calm. **"If they're on standby, they're likely waiting for another trigger. A tangible tension settles over the team. The bright, hopeful hum of the Ghtroc's newly awakened systems seems to dim slightly, becoming a more cautious, low-level thrum. Inside the cockpit, the console lights that had flared to life now settle into a more subdued, steady glow. The immediate threat of activation seems to have been averted, but the entire dynamic of their mission has shifted. They are no longer just exploring a dead ship; they are now intruders in a sleeping dragon's lair, trying desperately not to wake it.

Wes nodded as they reported in.  **”Copy that Marina.  Keep power levels as minimal as possible while allowing the ship's main computer to run.  What info we can pull off the main computer and this data pad could be important to what we need.   You and Echo sweep the rest of the ship from stern to bow.”** 


He looked over to Sera. **”Tell me some good news.  The ship's computer is helping you out on the data pad.  Yes?”**

Sera's fingers tap a rapid, almost frantic, rhythm on the console. The Ghtroc's main computer, ancient as it is, provides a processing power that dwarfs her portable kit. **"Good news? I've got good news,"** she says, a triumphant grin spreading across her face. **"The main computer core is in surprisingly good shape. 'The Pathfinder's Legacy'."** She looks up at Wes, the significance of the last phrase hanging in the air. **"Whatever they were looking for, it sounds a lot like the mystery we've stumbled into."**

Wes looked on as Sera flexed her considerable skill on the datapad.   Now with the added power of the transport's main computer the Zeltron was able to quickly access the logs. 


He listened as she read off some flight records and the crew list.  It was a small crew that had set out seventy-three years ago.  Only to never return.   With the state of this cargo bay it seemed obvious that this wasn’t their first foray here.

He shook his head slowly.   **”One more question to added to the mystery.   How does this ship and who they work for add into it all?  How many others are looking for the same thing?   Sera, keep digging.  See what else is in the logs.  Previous trips here.  What may have been found on those previous trips and anything mentioning Pathfinder or Wayfinder.  We’re on to something here.  The Force led us here.  Let’s see if we can unravel this mystery a bit more.  We’re still on a time frame with the main mission to finish.  Let’s not waste any time.”** 


He turned back to the controls and started making inquiries to the ship's main computer.   He entered search words Pathfinder, Wayfinder as well as the parts they were still needed on their mission list.

**"Copy that, Wes,"** Sera says, her focus narrowing. The cockpit falls into a focused silence, broken only by the soft hum of the newly activated systems and the rapid, almost silent tapping of Sera's and Wes's inputs. On the main display, files flicker open and close as the Ghtroc's ancient computer struggles to keep up with their tandem queries.

After a few minutes of intense searching, the computer pings with several partial hits.

`// Search Query: "Wayfinder" / "Pathfinder" //`
`Result(1): ...Project File: 'The Pathfinder's Legacy'... Access Restricted. Encryption Level: Archaic, Tier-9. Note: Linked to primary astrogation query...`
`Result(2): ...[CORRUPTED_ENTRY]... our own Pathfinder confirms the resonance. The signal is weak, but the vector is holding. We believe the source is the 'Shadow'...`
`Result(3): ...Astrogation Query Log... Route Designation: 'The Path'. Status: Calculation TIMEOUT. Prerequisite artifact signature: [NOT DETECTED]...`

The comm crackles to life with a report from the aft of the ship.

It also looked like the astrogation log needed an artifact signature.   There was something about this ship that seemed special but knowing more didn’t factor into the time they had.  


**”Sera, that project file designated ‘The Pathfinders Legacy’ looks to have some heavy duty encryption.  Can you crack that with the ship's computer?  More importantly, how long could it take?”** 


Before Sera could answer the comms crackled and Marina reported back with a status report.    The ship was indeed empty with no sign of the crew.  No evidence of a struggle.   They seem to have just disappeared.  And he wonders if the one body they found could have been one of the crew.  As Marina paused he quickly unslung the old rifle he had picked up and handed it to Sera.   **”I know you have a lot on your plate already. The power core looked like it could be fired up and the ship not just salvageable but viable.”**  he remained silent for long seconds as he ran a few thoughts through his head.

He paused again for a moment.  **”Alright we may need to change direction.  First are we closer to our next two objectives from where we are right now or is Kael closer to where he is. Sera takes the ancient rifle from Wes, her expert eyes immediately assessing its strange design and feel. Marina, however, looks at Wes, a flicker of deep concern in her eyes, a feeling that likely resonates through their bond. **"Wes... splitting the team is a massive risk,"** she says, her voice low and serious over their private channel, though her professional demeanor remains intact for the others. **"You'll have Roric, which is good, but you'll be down a scout and a dedicated slicer in a structurally compromised, unknown section of the ship. Wes gave Sera a nod as she started working on the comms issue.   The idea she had held some promise.   Comms wouldn’t be clear but potentially somewhat reliable. Echo’s calm demeanor was reliable as always.   He was relieved to see that she saw merit in the plan.   **”Lets make sure we all have the maps we’ve acquired and the projected path both teams are expected to make.   If either team has to deviate off the path we mark it on the bulkhead.”**


When he turned and looked at Marina he wasn’t ready for the look he saw in her eyes.  The deep concern he felt through the Force and their bond hit like a hammer.  She was a voice of reason and it was one of the things he loved her for.   Seeing her hand absentmindedly go to her abdomen and he understood her worry.    


He stepped closer and breaking any sense of professional forum he placed his own hand over hers.   The tingle that they had both come to expect from contact was greatly subdued due to what they wore but it was still there.  He took a breath and slowed down his thinking.  As he did he reached out with the Force to his love.  Reassuring emotions flowed out and encompassed her.     They were moving fast.  While there was no mission timer they didn’t necessarily have time to plant stakes and make themselves at home.  There was also no need to rush aimlessly ahead.

He nodded and spoke his comms going out to the group.  **"I know it’s risky, Marina.  The plan on splitting the team is calculated.  That’s what I split it the way I did. He looked around at the others.  **”Alright team.  Sera, keep working on your idea for the comms.   Echo, check the maps we have for both locations.  Plot what seems to be the best course for both. Check what we have and if there may be anything of use here on the freighter.  Marina you and I will go over the *Moon* and make sure she’s as ready to go as possible.”** 


He took a deep breath.  **”If anyone finds or comes across anything that throws this plan into the compactor speak up.  Let me know.”**  He gave those assembled a nod.  **”Alright let’s get to work.”**

Wes's calm, decisive commands settle over the team, replacing the initial shock of the audacious new plan with a focused sense of purpose. The calculated risk has been acknowledged, and now the professionals get to work.

**"Copy, Wes,"** Sera confirms, her attention already shifting from the Ghtroc's main computer back to its comms console. **"I'll reroute auxiliary power from the core systems to the main comms array. Marina's initial concern softens under the reassuring pressure of Wes's hand and the steady calm he projects through their bond. The team dissolves into a flurry of quiet, efficient activity. The cockpit of the *Seventh Moon* is filled with the soft tapping of Sera's inputs. The main hold becomes a temporary command center as Echo plots routes on her datapad. Roric methodically organizes their limited heavy weapons. And in the dark, dusty corridor leading to the engine room, Wes and Marina, working side-by-side, begin the monumental task of determining if this long-dead ghost of a ship can truly be brought back to life.

Wes follows Marina to the Ghtroc's engine room. The space is cramped, a maze of cold, silent machinery coated in a thick blanket of dust. Marina's suit light plays over the power core, a complex assembly of conduits and converters.

**"Okay,"** she begins, her voice echoing slightly in the confined space. **"The good news is, the Cryo-Cycle core is a closed system. As long as the containment hasn't been breached, the primary fuel should be stable, even after all this time."** She runs a hand-scanner over a series of diagnostic ports, her visor's display reflecting complex energy readings. **"Auxiliary power is holding steady. They spend the next hour in a focused, almost silent dance of diagnostics. Wes, following Marina's direction, helps test physical components, his strength useful in turning seized valves or moving heavy access plates. Marina, the surgeon, probes the ship's electronic guts, her mind a whirlwind of calculations and bypass theories.

Finally, she straightens up, wiping a dusty glove across her helmet's visor. **"Alright, here's the verdict,"** she says, her tone a mixture of exhaustion and a mechanic's pride. **"She's not going to win any races. The main engines need a full overhaul we can't possibly do here. But... the repulsorlifts are viable. It will take every bit of juice from my portable starter and a very specific, very careful power-up sequence to kickstart the fusion cycle, but it *can* be done."**

She looks at Wes, the seriousness of the situation clear in her voice. **"So, yes. She can fly. We can get her out of this hangar bay and limp her back to the *Chu'unthor*. But it's going to be a one-shot deal. Once we start the main power-up sequence, it's going to be a massive energy spike. It will alert every dormant system on the *Veiled Shadow*. From the moment we decide to fire her up, we'll be on a very short, very loud clock to get out of here before those 'janitor' droids come calling."**

As Wes worked alongside Marina in engineering.   It was no surprise that she was in her element as he watched a master in her craft work.   More than once he saw her back track when she either found or thought of something.  It spoke to her thoroughness to make sure that the *Seventh Moon* would fly.   


Throughout the next hour they world hand in hand.  Several times he received updates from the others on their progress.   Things seemed to be going well with the prep for the split of their forces.   It was a calculated risk.   One that none of them were expecting.  


Finally after they both picked up some grime from digging into engines and anything dirty they both stood up.  He watched as she wiped her dirty and dusty glove across her visor.  She was focused and tired and she had never looked more beautiful.   As she went over things he gave her a knowing smile, one that he used only for her.  


The *Seventh Moon* was viable.   She would need some tender care especially as they navigated the expanse to normal space as well as the trip home but she would make it.    The only problem was firing up her engines would alert the whole ship.  **”Maybe I can talk to the ship.   Let it know that we’re not a threat that needs to be cleaned.  Maybe it will understand that we’re not a threat.  If not that, maybe a few extra minutes to blast out of here.”**   


He stepped close and took her hand in his.  **”Good work.  From the reports the others have sent and what you’ve done here.  I think we’re looking good.”**

Marina's tired face breaks into a small, appreciative smile as Wes takes her hand. The simple gesture, the shared confidence, is a welcome counterpoint to the grease, dust, and daunting technical challenges they've just navigated. She squeezes his hand in return.

**"Talking to the ship?"** she repeats, considering his idea. The professional mechanic in her is skeptical, but the Jedi who witnessed his connection to the Wayfinder and the ship's own systems is more open-minded. **"It's possible. Your connection... it seems to be the only thing this ancient system responds to non-aggressively. If you could somehow... broadcast a 'stand-down' or 'friendly' signal through the Force as we power up, it might delay the security response. Confuse it, maybe. Give us those extra few minutes we'll desperately need."**

She lets out a slow breath, the reality of the next phase settling in. **"It's our best shot, Wes. Better than just hoping we're faster than a horde of angry, ancient droids."** Her gaze is steady, her trust in his unique abilities clear. **"Alright. Let's sync up with the others and finalize the timeline. We've got two objectives to secure before we can even think about starting this engine."**

He smiled at her confidence in him.   He could see as well as feel it from her.   She was right.   Any extra time he could manage to get from the *Shadow* may be time they desperately needed when they fire up the *Moon.*  It sounded like the best shot they had.  


He nodded as she suggested they head back to meet up with the others.  **”Let's go.”**  He turned and walked beside her back up to the cockpit.  There the others were waiting and conversing about the upcoming mission parameters and the split that was involved.  **”Well the *Moon.* is a go.  When it’s time to go she will get off the *Shadow.*  She won’t win any races but she can make it back home.”**  He looked around to the others. **”How did the rest of the plans go?”**

Echo, who had been studying the newly uploaded route overlays with a critical eye, looks up as Wes and Marina return. **"Routes are plotted and distributed, Wes,"** she reports, her tone efficient. **"Team Skimmer has a primary and two alternate paths to the bridge access point on Deck 2. The maintenance corridor is our best bet; it's tight but bypasses the main droid patrols Sera flagged. Team Moon's route to engineering sub-level 4 is more direct but has that structural integrity warning. You'll need to be cautious there."**

Roric, having finished organizing the gear, nods. **"Ordnance is split. One ion rifle and two grenades per team. I've also packed extra power cells I located for both groups. We're as equipped as we can be."**

Sera turns from the console she's been monitoring, a satisfied look on her face. **"And the comms relay is prepped,"** she announces. **"As soon as Marina gets the *Moon's* main comms powered up, I can establish our secure, low-power link to the *Skimmer*. It'll be a fragile connection, but it's our only line between the two teams."**

She pauses, then looks at the whole group. **"So, the *Seventh Moon* is a go? You can actually fly this thing out of here?"** The question is directed at Wes and Marina, her voice a mix of a slicer's professional skepticism and a refugee's dawning hope.

Wes nodded as Echo and Roric as they reported in.  Both as expected came though with what he had asked for.   A plotted path to each destination and their available gear redistributed between the two teams.  **”Thank you both.  Good work.”**


Sera was quick to turn in her chair.   The red skinned Zeltron wore a satisfied smile as she reported that she comms relay prepped.  It wouldn’t be perfect but it would allow for, in her words, ‘a fragile’ connection.   She paused before she spoke again.   Her next question didn’t surprise him.  The current state of the *Moon.* 


He gave her a nod and spoke.  **”Marina has had to make some routing changes for power and we’ve only got one shot with her.   When she’s fired up there won’t be any shutting her down and starting again.  One and done.   She can better explain it.  The concern is the massive power spike that will light us up to the ‘janitor’ droids.  To combat that I will attempt to communicate to ships' consciousness again.  Communicating that we're not a threat and that we’re leaving.  Hoping that if it doesn’t stop them it will slow the response time giving us a few more minutes to get the hell out.  I think that both ships powering up at the same time would help draw any activated ‘janitor’ droids into smaller groups.   The plan being that we don’t deal with them at all.   If we need to I suggest using the ships cannons to deal with the droids.”**

**"Alright, Sera,"** Marina's voice cuts through the quiet, all business. **"I'm diverting the auxiliary power feed to the main comms array now. It'll be a trickle, just enough for you to establish the low-power relay. We'll only be able to send short data-bursts back and forth with the *Skimmer* until we fire up the main core."** She immediately gets to work, her hands flying over a newly activated engineering console.
**"Copy, Marina. Standing by to establish the handshake,"** Sera replies, her own focus absolute as she prepares the fragile communication link that will be their only connection.

Echo gives a final, sharp nod to Wes. **"Team Skimmer is ready to move on your command, Wes. We'll see you on the other side."** With that, she turns, her movements economical and silent, and heads for the cargo hatch, her carbine held at a low ready.

Without a word, Sera follows Echo, her slicing kit secured, her expression a mask of professional determination. The two of them disappear through the hatch and back into the vast, silent hangar of the *Veiled Shadow*, their suit lights quickly swallowed by the oppressive darkness as they begin the long trek back to the *Void Skimmer*.

Roric Jannsen shoulders his ion rifle, his gaze meeting Wes's. He gives a single, curt nod – a soldier's acknowledgment of a shared, dangerous path ahead. **"We've got your back down here, Del-Fin,"** he grunts, then turns to head back to the *Moon's* hatch.

The team has split. A profound sense of separation settles over the two remaining members in the cockpit. They are now two small, isolated groups in a vast, ancient tomb, their survival dependent on skill, luck, and a fragile, jury-rigged comms link.

Marina watches the tactical icons representing Sera and Echo begin their slow, steady trek across the hangar schematic on her datapad. She takes a final, steadying breath, her professional focus pushing aside the momentary feeling of separation.

**"Alright,"** she says, her voice crisp as she stands. **"Comms relay is active and stable for now."** She gives Wes a final, determined look, a silent acknowledgment of the risks they are both now taking. **"Let's go get those power converters."**

With that, she turns and follows the path Roric took, her own suit light cutting a clean, focused beam into the dusty, silent corridor of the *Seventh Moon*, leaving Wes for a moment in the quiet, humming glow of the cockpit. She moves with purpose, ready to descend deeper into the heart of the *Veiled Shadow*.

His team enacted the plan.  The comms relay was coming up as Echo and Sera made ready to depart.   He could feel the concern at the separation of the team but he also felt the confidence they felt in his plan.  That they felt that way raised his own confidence in the direction he had chosen for his people.  It wasn’t an easy choice but he felt it was the best choice to complete both objectives.   He stood as Echo spoke to their readiness.   He reached out to both in turn.  **”Good luck and may the Force be with you.”**  He watched as they turned the corner and were out of sight.  They were on their way to get Kael and head to the bridge. 


That left Roric, Marina and himself in the cockpit.  Roric, a man of few words, reinforced that they were behind him.  The vote of confidence from the experienced soldier drew a simple nod of thanks from Wes as he watched him turn and head towards the *Moon’s* hatch.  


That left Marina and him alone in the now seemingly large cockpit.  As she looked up from her datapad her voice rang out clear, crisp and determined.   He gave her a nod as she turned to follow Roric.  Leaving him alone in the cockpit.  It was then he had a feeling of what his Father and countless other leaders have felt throughout history.   The loneliness of command.   That giving orders no matter how well thought out could have drastic consequences on those you cared about.  


He took a deep breath as his eyes fell on the old rifle he found.  He remembered its balance and how it felt in his hands.  He wished the energy cell wasn’t depleted but he knew wishes wouldn't change that.  He opened a new comms channel to Marina and Roric.  **”On my way to you.   I”ll take point.  Marina second, Roric brings up the rear and covers us like he has been all along.  Eyes and ears open.  Let’s do this by the numbers.”**

The team falls into a tight, professional formation. Wes takes point, his helmet's light cutting a path into the oppressive darkness of the engineering sub-level. Marina follows, her scanner now active, its beam playing over the ancient conduits and support structures, while Roric brings up the rear, his heavy rifle a constant, silent presence covering their back. The corridor here is different—wider, more industrial, lined with massive, silent machinery and thick, insulated pipes that sweat a thin film of ancient, frozen condensation. The only sound is the soft hiss of their suit recyclers.

The schematics guide them true, and after several minutes of tense, silent progress, they arrive at the section marked with the structural integrity warning. The corridor ahead opens into a vast, cavernous chamber that must have once been a primary engineering hub. And here, their path ends abruptly.

A huge section of the deck plating has given way, collapsing into the pitch-black void of the deck below. A chasm, at least twenty meters across, yawns before them, a silent wound in the heart of the ship. The far side, where the corridor continues towards their objective, seems impossibly distant. The sheer drop disappears into a darkness so complete their powerful suit lights cannot find the bottom.

Wes's tactical mind immediately assesses the options. Spanning the gap are two massive, but badly corroded, support beams, their surfaces slick with the same frozen condensation as the pipes. A skilled climber might be able to traverse them, but a single slip would mean a long, silent fall. Further to the left, a thick bundle of heavy power conduits, still secured to the ceiling, sags across the chasm, offering a potential hand-over-hand crossing for someone strong enough to attempt it. Alternatively, the schematic on his HUD shows this chamber is vast; a detour around the chasm might be possible, but finding a safe, intact maintenance tunnel or alternate causeway in this un-scouted, damaged section would be a time-consuming and uncertain gamble.

Wes stood before the twenty meter chasm.  He was unsure of the depth as their lights didn’t penetrate that far down.  Either way to fall was to meet one’s end.  The options they had weren’t very good either.  Traverse corroded support beams that were slick with ice or sagging power conduits that would require going hand over hand.  Neither seemed prudent or practical.  **”Structural integrity warning was no joke.”**   He looked at Marina and Roric.   **”Ice on the support beams or hand over hand across the power conduits.  Or I could fly each of you over, one at a time.   I’m for option three myself.”**

Marina's suit light plays over Roric, his bulky frame and heavy gear a stark contrast to her own, then back to Wes. Through their bond, he feels her rapid calculation, a mix of technical assessment and concern.

**"Option three is the safest for getting across,"** she agrees over the comm, her voice practical. **"But Wes... your pack's lift capacity. It can handle me and my gear, no problem. But Roric... he's well over the safety margin for a tandem flight, especially with this kind of open drop."**

She shifts her light to the thick power conduits hanging from the ceiling. **"The best plan would be for you to fly across and find a solid anchor point. I've got a hundred meters of high-tensile synth-cable and a climbing harness in my kit. Once you secure the line, and then Roric can use it as a safety line to traverse one of those support beams. It's much safer than him trying to balance on that ice unassisted."**

Roric, who had been silently assessing the icy beams with a grim expression, grunts in agreement. **"That works. A safety line makes the beam viable."** He then pans his light across the chasm to the corridor on the other side, his practical mind already thinking several steps ahead. **"That handles us getting across. But what about the return trip? Those power converters we're after are heavy. The lifter droids can't cross this. We'll need to figure out how to get the salvage back over this gap before we go too much further."**

Wes looked from the chasm to Marina and Roric as she expressed an idea of a safety line and harness to traverse the corroded and icy support beams.   He nodded as Roric supported the idea.   The soldier’s pragmatic comments about coming back with the bulky and heavy power converters and issue with the chasm.   


He shifted his gaze to Marina.  **”The two of us use the Force and lift the power converters and the lifter droids across.”**  He looked across the divide.   **”The two of us share the burden.”**  He nodded.  **”We can do it.  This way we still have the lifter droids to get the cargo back to the *Moon*.”**

Marina considers Wes's bold suggestion. Using the Force to levitate the heavy power converters *and* the lifter droids across a twenty-meter chasm is an ambitious undertaking, a significant feat of telekinesis, especially for an Initiate and a Padawan, and doubly so given her pregnancy.

**"Lift them across..."** she repeats, her voice thoughtful. **"The converters will be dense and heavy. The lifter droids... less dense, but awkward. It's... possible, Wes. If we work together, combining our strength through the Force... yes."** Through their bond, he can feel her initial surprise give way to a determined resolve. She trusts him, and she trusts their connection. **"It would be a strain, but you're right. It's the most viable way to get the salvage back. We share the burden."**

She gives a firm nod, her focus now on the immediate plan. **"Alright. Get across and set that anchor. We need to get moving."**

Roric remains silent, having no expertise in the esoteric possibilities of the Force. He simply gives a curt, single nod, accepting the plan. If the Jedi say they can move mountains (or in this case, multi-ton salvage), his job is to trust them and cover them while they do it.

Wes accepted the synth-cable that Marina offered. **”I know it will be heavy but we can lighten the load by taking the salvage separately from the haulers. It will mean more reps executing the strategy but less effort all around.”**  


He turned back to the expanse that they needed to get across.  They had a plan; they just needed to put it into motion.  The confidence he felt from Marina was comforting as he gave them both a nod.   **”I’ll go over and tie it off.  Then bring the other end back.”**


Taking a few steps to the side of his two companions.  Wes activated the controls of his jetpack and felt it rumble against his back as the exhaust lifted his heels off the deck plating for a second.  **”Be right back.  Roric find a good place to anchor the end I bring back”**  A second later he was airborne and crossing the chasm slowly.   It was only twenty meters so there was no need for speed, just a controlled flight or a long jump depending on how one looked at it.   


Seconds later his feet touched down on the other side and looked around to make sure the area was secure.  **”The area over here looks secure.  Going to see about anchoring this rope.”**  In short order he was once again crossing the gap after securing the line so Roric could use it to help himself across the beam.   The line unfurled from his hands as he flew back.  Touching down gently he handed the rope to Roric.  **”Got a spot?”**

Roric takes the end of the high-tensile synth-cable from Wes, his armored glove closing around it with a firm grip. While Wes was securing the other end, Roric had been examining the area near the chasm's edge.

**"Here,"** he grunts, gesturing with his head towards the base of a massive, structurally sound support pillar a few feet back from the precipice. **"It's an integrated part of the primary superstructure. Won't budge. I'll secure this end with a locking carabiner and a double tension knot. It'll hold."**

He moves to the pillar and, with practiced efficiency, begins anchoring the line, creating a taught, reliable safety tether that stretches across the dark abyss to the anchor point Wes established on the far side.

Marina watches Roric expertly secure the safety line, her professional respect for his competence evident in her posture. Once the line is taut and ready, she turns to Wes, giving him a firm, expectant nod. **"Alright, Wes. I'm ready."** She moves to his side, her gaze fixed on the far side of the chasm, her trust in him and his piloting absolute.

Wes adjusts his stance, and with practiced ease, wraps a secure arm around Marina, mindful of her balance and the gear she carries. The jetpack roars to life with a controlled, powerful burst, lifting them both smoothly from the deck. The flight across the twenty-meter gap is steady and uneventful, Wes's control over the pack precise. He sets Marina down gently on the far side, her boots touching the deck with barely a sound.

As they land, they both turn to look back across the chasm. Roric is already halfway across one of the slick, icy support beams. He moves with a slow, deliberate, and surprisingly steady gait for a man in heavy armor. One hand maintains a firm grip on the synth-cable safety line, sliding it along as he progresses, while the other holds his heavy rifle at a low ready, his gaze constantly scanning the darkness below and ahead. His progress is careful but confident, the movements of a veteran who trusts his gear and his training implicitly. In a matter of minutes, his heavy boots step off the beam and onto the solid deck plating beside them.

Wes lowered the two of them in a controlled descent.   They weren’t very high but even the slightest of mistakes could cause them to come down hard.  Neither of them needed that.  Especially on an ancient derelict a long way from home.  As he set Marina down gently he smiled.  **”Just like last time. Well, this time the jet pack is in good working order.”** His smile could be heard through their comms.     


Together they turned to check on Roric’s progress.   Wes wasn’t surprised to see the veteran soldier already half way across the support beam.  It spoke to the man’s skill and confidence in his ability.  When Roric’s boots landed on the solid decking Wes gave the man a nod of respect.  **”Nicey done Ror.”**  He said as he brought up his bracer computer.    The display once again shows the path to engineering.   Only this time they were on the opposite side of the chasm.   


**”Same as before.  Let’s keep moving.  If you see or sense something, speak up.”**  Wes reached down and drew his blaster that was holstered at his side.  With his eyes and Force senses open.  He headed in the direction displayed on the map.

With Roric across, the team is reunited. Wes gives the order, and they fall into formation, moving away from the chasm and deeper into the engineering sub-levels of the colossal derelict. The architecture here shifts. The utilitarian service corridors give way to a wider, more industrial passage, lined with massive, silent machinery and thick, insulated pipes that sweat a thin, ancient film of frozen condensation. The only light is from their suits, cutting sharp beams through the absolute darkness. The only sound is the soft, private hum of their own life support.

As they follow the glowing route on their HUDs, the subtle Force resonance that Wes and Marina felt earlier intensifies. It's no longer a distant echo but a palpable, ambient thrum that seems to vibrate in the very deck plating beneath their boots, a constant pressure against their senses.

They soon enter a section of the corridor marked on their HUDs with the warning Marina had found: `// Warning: Structural Integrity Compromised in Sector 4-G. //` The evidence is immediate and unsettling. The deck is no longer level, tilting at a precarious angle. Massive stress fractures, like jagged black lightning, run up the walls and across the ceiling. Heaps of dust and small, metallic fragments litter the floor, clearly having fallen from above. The entire section groans under unimaginable strain, the sound a low, tortured vibration transmitted directly through the soles of their boots. Moving forward requires careful, deliberate steps, navigating buckled plates and avoiding the most obviously unstable sections of the ceiling.

As they traversed the path the feeling from the Force that he had come to understand as danger began to creep up within him.  The corridor out before the team started to become degraded and the path had become treacherous.  Stress fractures and metal shavings crunched beneath their feet as they walked.   **”This isn’t good.   We won’t be able to come back this way with cargo haulers.  Another route will need to be found or we won’t be bringing anything back.”**   


He looked at the map Echo had drawn up based on what info they had available.   Looking to see if there was another route.  **”Checking to see if Echo may have planned an alternate route for us.  Find a solid spot, keep an eye on the ceiling and floor.  This could get bad quickly.”**  He advised knowing they were already aware of the situation.

**"Copy that, Wes,"** Marina's voice replies, a note of professional concern in her tone. Her suit light is already playing over the jagged stress fractures in the bulkhead, her mechanic's eye assessing the strain. **"The hull integrity in this section is definitely compromised. The stress is radiating from that upper joint. It's stable for now, but any significant vibration... it could trigger a collapse. Let's move carefully and keep our distance from that wall."**

**"Agreed,"** Roric grunts, his own heavy footsteps now noticeably more deliberate, testing each section of the deck plating before committing his full weight. His rifle is up, his light scanning the ceiling, watching for any sign of shifting debris or further structural failure. **"Finding an alternate return route is now a primary concern."**

Wes pulls up the route Echo had plotted on his HUD. He can see the primary path she laid out, the one they are currently on, which continues through this hazardous sector. However, her thoroughness is evident. A secondary, fainter line branches off from their current position, leading into what the schematics label as a series of smaller, parallel maintenance conduits. It's a more circuitous route, clearly tighter and more complex than this main passage, but it bypasses the worst of the marked structural damage ahead. Finding the access hatch for these conduits, however, would require a sharp eye amidst the debris and failing light.

**”Agreed Roric.  This path is a no go.”** He said as he was looking at the path on the HUD in his helmet.   He spotted another path that Echo had paid out.   It was more circular but it took them out of the way of all the structural damage that lay before them.   **”Echo”s alternate path she plotted is here.  It looks good.  We will bypass this compromised section.  If a more roundabout route, it looks like it gets tight but it should be navigable.”**   He looked up at jis two companions.  **”Its maintenance conduits and the access panel should be around here somewhere.   Help me look.”**  


Wes turned to the wall letting the lights from his armor shift on the ancient  metal that compromised the hull.  His eyes and senses opened as he looked for the signs of a long forgotten maintenance hatch.

Following the path indicated on their HUDs, Wes begins a methodical scan of the corridor wall, his helmet light cutting a sharp, focused beam across the corroded metal. Marina and Roric do the same, their lights sweeping the area, searching for any break in the smooth, dust-covered surface.

After a moment of searching, Wes's sharp eyes catch it. Partially obscured by a fallen piece of ceiling panel and caked in a thick layer of grime is a faint, rectangular outline, almost invisible against the surrounding bulkhead. It's a standard, if ancient, maintenance access hatch, its manual release wheel seized tight with rust and age. It's exactly where Echo's alternate route indicated an entry point should be.

His helmet light cut through the darkness as they fanned out in search of the maintenance hatch.  **”According to the plans Echo prepped it should be right around here.”**   


It didn’t take long.  As the beam of light cut across a fallen ceiling panel he caught the out light of a partially covered rectangular outline.  If it hadn’t been for the bright light illuminating the area he would have missed it.  **”I may have something here.  Roric, give me a hand.”**  He asked and between the two of them they easily managed to move the debris from the hatch.   Placing a hand on the release wheel he felt that it was seized.   Holstering his blaster he placed both hands on the wheel and attempted to turn it.

Roric grunts in acknowledgment and moves to help Wes. Together, they grip the edges of the heavy, twisted ceiling panel. With a coordinated effort and a groan of protesting metal, they heave the debris aside, fully exposing the ancient maintenance hatch.

Wes holsters his blaster and plants his feet, gripping the seized release wheel with both armored hands. He puts his full strength into it, his muscles straining against a mechanism that hasn't moved in a thousand lifetimes. The wheel doesn't budge. He resets his grip, taking a deeper stance, and tries again, channeling his strength with a steady, controlled force.

For a long, tense moment, there is only the sound of his own breathing over the comms. Then, with a series of sharp, protesting **CRACKS** like breaking bones, the rusted locking mechanism finally gives way. The wheel turns, stiffly at first, then more smoothly. With a final, heavy **CLUNK**, the hatch is unlocked.

Wes pulls, and the heavy door swings inwards with a low, groaning screech, revealing a dark, narrow passage beyond – the maintenance conduit Echo had identified on the schematics. A gust of stale, inert gas, smelling faintly of old lubricants and ozone, wafts silently out into the vacuum of the main corridor.

The maintenance conduit is a stark contrast to the cavernous corridor they just left. It's a tight, rectangular tunnel, barely wide enough for Roric to pass through without turning sideways. Pipes and thick bundles of ancient, insulated cables line the walls and ceiling, leaving little room to maneuver. The air here, trapped for millennia, is stale and thin, but their suit sensors confirm it's a stable, non-toxic mix of inert gases.

The team falls into a single-file line: Wes, followed closely by Marina, with Roric bringing up the rear, his broad frame almost scraping the walls. Their progress is slower, more deliberate, their suit lights illuminating only a few meters of the cramped passage ahead at a time. The path twists and turns, a labyrinth of right-angle bends and short, straightaways.

After fifteen minutes of tense, claustrophobic navigation, the conduit opens into a small maintenance junction. And here, they find another body.

This one is slumped against a wall, clad in the same archaic, non-Republic uniform as the skeleton in the hangar. Like the other, it is a skeletal form, long past identification. But this one is different. In its lap, held by skeletal fingers, is a datapad – its screen shattered, but its casing seemingly intact. And clutched in its other hand is a single, elegant tool: a plasma torch. Scattered around the body are several spent, discharged energy cells of an unfamiliar design.

Marina is the first to speak, her voice a low murmur over the comms. **"Another one... from the *Seventh Moon* crew, it has to be."** She moves closer, her scanner playing over the scene. **"Look at the bulkhead behind him."**

The team's lights converge on the wall. A large, rectangular section of the bulkhead has been crudely, but expertly, cut away and then messily re-welded back into place. The scorch marks are deep, and the welds are thick and uneven.

**"He was trying to cut through the wall,"** Marina concludes, her voice filled with a dawning understanding. **"He used up all his power cells trying to get through, and then... he just sat down and died here. But why? What was he trying to get to?"** She points her scanner at the re-welded panel. **"There's something on the other side. A massive power source. It's... it's the main reactor chamber for this sub-level. He was trying to cut his way *into* engineering."**

Wes looked at the new pathway.  It was going to be tight and there was no way they were going to get the salvage through it.  He looked back at Marina and Roric.  **”Single file.  If we find that salvage we’re looking for we won’t be coming back this way.”**  He said matter of factly as he drew his blaster and turned his shoulders.   With a confident first step he entered the narrow maintainer conduit.  


The next quarter hour was a combination of twisting and turning as they traversed the path on his HUD.   There was little talk as they all worked to keep moving as he kept aware of his two companions.  


Finally the cramped confines of the maintainer conduit opened up into a small junction that had turned into a tomb.   Slumped against the wall was another body dressed in the same uniform as the other they had found.  Wes moved forward to visually scan the conduit ahead as Marina looked over the body.   Coming back he saw the broken data pad.   


**”I think you’re right Marina.   Same uniform as the other.  This makes two.  There should be two more somewhere.”**   When she pointed out the bulkhead he knelt down to inspect the weld.  He listened as he spoke.  Her scanner detected a massive power source on the other side.  


**”If he was trying to get in, maybe his other two companions we haven’t found yet were trapped in there.  Maybe he was injured and his last moments of life were trying to rescue his companions.”**  It was a sobering thought as he knew in a similar circumstance he’d do the same for the others.

**”Marina, can you get a sense off the data pad of the user's last moments?  Maybe there’s some sort of Force resonance left.  Like the crystal on Fakir.”**  He wasn’t sure if that would work as the crystal was a kyber crystal used in a lightsaber.   They were attuned to the Force.  **”I’ll see if I can sense anything on the other side.  If there’s something in there we could possibly cut our way in through the bulkhead if we need to.”**


From his kneeling position he looked at Roric and gave their ever vigilant companion a nod.  He then placed his hand upon the wall, closing his eyes.  Slowly he pushed out with his senses, past the bulkhead into the area beyond.

Marina shakes her head, the motion visible through her visor. **"I can try, Wes, but it's not like the crystal,"** she explains over the comm, her voice tinged with a hint of regret. **"Psychometry, for me, usually requires direct skin contact. This suit... it acts as a buffer. I might get a faint, muddled impression of strong emotions, but nothing clear like a vision or a specific memory. Still..."** She kneels beside the body and, despite the barrier of her glove, gently places her hand over the shattered datapad, closing her eyes in concentration, trying to sense any lingering echo in the Force.

As Marina attempts her psychometry, Wes places his own gloved hand on the rough, re-welded section of the bulkhead. He closes his eyes and extends his senses, pushing his awareness through the thick metal and into the chamber beyond – the main reactor room for this sub-level.

The first thing that hits him is the sheer *scale* of the space. It's immense, a multi-story chamber filled with colossal, silent machinery. But overpowering everything is the Force resonance. It's the same deep, ancient, powerful hum they felt from the crystal, but here it is magnified a hundredfold, a deafening, silent roar of dormant energy. This is its source.

And then he feels something else.

Amidst the overwhelming power, he senses two more voids. Two more cold, empty spots in the Force, identical to the dormant droids. They are not in the main reactor chamber, but in a shielded annex. They are silent sentinels, guarding the prize.

Reaching out the initial size of the space surprised him.  It was massive.  Multiple stories and filled with massive equipment that was silent as if asleep.    The reactor room for the sub-level felt more like a tomb than anything else.   


Within the space he felt it.   Much like it was back when they found the first crystal he felt the Force resonance.  It was different this time.  What was a strong resonance earlier was now dwarfed to what he was feeling now.   He could only imagine the size of the crystal that was in there.   If it was a crystal at all.   He could feel the resonant hum in the Force out here past the bulkhead now that he was aware of it.   


He shifted his focus to try and tune out some of the noise or silence depending on how one looked at things.   It was then he felt the other presences or voids as he had come to know the massive cleaner droids.   They weren’t in the main chamber but a shielded section.   Silent in their protective vigil.   


He removed his hand from the bulkhead and let his armored forearm rest on his knee.  **”It’s a massive chamber in there.  Multiple stories and massive machinery.   It’s silent in there.   There’s two of the cleaner droids in there. They aren’t in the main reaction chamber but a shrieked annex of sorts.   There one other thing,”**  He paused as he looked up at Roric and then back to her.   **”The Force resonance in there is massive.  Compared to what we felt back with the other crystal this is easily, a hundred times that.  It’s sleeping.”**  He looked at the wall where the hasty weld job was done but the long dead crew member.   **”Maybe he was trying to seal it off to keep something in.”**

Marina pulls her hand back from the shattered datapad, a slight shake of her head visible. **"Nothing,"** she reports, her voice tinged with frustration. **"Too much interference from my suit, and the device is too degraded. I can't get a clear reading."**

She turns her full attention to Wes as he describes what he's sensed on the other side of the wall. Her eyes widen behind her visor at the mention of two more droids and the overwhelming Force resonance. **"A hundred times stronger?"** she breathes, her gaze fixed on the crude weld marks. **"Wes, if the resonance is that powerful even in a dormant state... it must be the primary power source for this entire section of the ship, if not the whole vessel. The crystal in the storage bay was just a secondary capacitor or a distribution node."**

She considers his final, chilling thought. **"Sealing it off..."** she murmurs, her suit light playing over the rough, melted edges of the cut. **"That's possible. If the droids in there activated, and the crew was trying to contain them... this might have been a desperate, last-ditch effort to trap them inside the reactor complex."** She looks back down the dark maintenance conduit they just traversed. **"Which means the 'safe' route Echo plotted might lead us right into the heart of their nest."**

Wes nodded as Marina spoke.   Her assumption of this power source aligned with his own.   This could be the main power supply for the entire ship.   That meant they were progressing into a potentially very dangerous area.  


**”I know.”** He agreed as he looked at Echo’s plotted route on the screen of his bracer computer.  **”We may not have a choice in the matter.  If the path takes us through there we have to go.”**  Concern for her safety rose as he looked back to the hastily laid weld marks.   He looked at Marina knowing what she would say if he proposed a different approach.   They took this mission knowing the dangers.  He knew that through their bond, she could sense his concern.  


He nodded, solidifying his resolve to see the mission through.  **”Alright let’s keep going.   If Echo’s path takes us around this chamber, good.  If we have to go through we do it by the numbers.  So far the droids have left us alone. They still should if we keep doing what we’re doing, not causing any power fluctuations.”**  He stood offering a hand to Marina.  **”Ready?”**  He looked at Roric and nodded and then looked back to Marina.  


**”Let's move.”**  He redrew his blaster, looked at the map and started off again along the tight and cramped maintenance conduit.

Roric grunts in acknowledgment, hefting his ion rifle. The prospect of walking into a room with two more dormant killer droids and an overwhelmingly powerful, unknown energy source doesn't seem to faze him outwardly. He simply falls into his rear-guard position, his helmet light scanning the dark passage behind them as they prepare to move on. **"Ready,"** is his only comment.

Marina takes Wes's offered hand, allowing him to help her to her feet. Through their bond, she feels the steel in his resolve, his determination to see the mission through despite the escalating risks and his personal concern for her. It strengthens her own resolve. She gives his hand a firm squeeze before letting go. **"Ready,"** she echoes, her voice clear and steady. She falls into step behind him, her scanner once again active, her focus absolute.

The team proceeds, leaving the silent tomb of the fallen crewmember behind. The maintenance conduit continues its twisting path. As they move, the ambient hum in the Force, the resonance from the main reactor, remains a constant, oppressive presence. The air in their suits feels heavy with it.

After another ten minutes of claustrophobic, tense progress, the conduit ends. A final hatch, this one unlocked, opens into a new space. They emerge into a wider, two-level engineering control room. Catwalks ring the upper level, looking down onto a central area filled with massive, dark computer consoles and what looks like a primary diagnostic pit. The air is still, silent, and thick with the same ancient dust.

According to their HUDs, this room is the antechamber to the main engineering section. A set of heavy blast doors on the far side of the lower level is marked as the entrance to the power converter storage annex they've been seeking.
