He leaned forward to be closer and took her gloved hand within his.  **”Yes we did Cyar’ika.”**  He said quietly as he watched the reflection of the star's streak past in blue eyes.   His own feeling of relief mixed with hers.  Relief that the mission was successful.  Everyone was safe and they had several unexpected surprises.   There was also a lot to unpack.   The Pathfinder and his promise to him.  To bring the Wayfinder or Key as it was referenced to back to the *Veil.*  He was going to have to convince Chase and the council of the importance of the discovery.  That he himself was also a Pathfinder.   


He gave Marina's hand a squeeze.   **”We couldn’t have done this without the team we brought.  Each one of us had a job to do.  Now we just need to get them to let me come back with the Wayfinder.”**  He took a deep breath and smiled at her.  **”First we get some well deserved rest.”**

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
Marina's smile softens as Wes takes her hand, the simple contact a comforting anchor after the adrenaline of the mission. She leans her head back against the co-pilot's seat, her eyes fluttering closed for a moment as the bone-deep exhaustion she'd been holding at bay finally begins to wash over her.

**"Rest..."** she murmurs, her voice thick with fatigue. **"That sounds like the best idea in the galaxy right now."** She opens her eyes again, focusing on him, her expression a mixture of pride and weariness. **"The team was solid. Roric, Echo... they were professional. And Sera is a miracle worker with old tech. You chose well, Wes. You *led* well."**

She hears his last comment, about returning with the Wayfinder, and a slight frown of confusion touches her features. She felt the weight of his experience on the bridge through their bond, the sense of awe and destiny, but she doesn't have the context for it yet.

**"Return with the Wayfinder?"** she asks, her voice a little hazy with tiredness. **"What... what did you mean? What happened on the bridge, Wes, after you sent us away? You said the Pathfinder was... gone. What did it say to you? What did you learn?"** She shifts in her seat, trying to push back the encroaching exhaustion, her curiosity and deep concern for him overriding her own need for sleep.

Wes smiled when he felt her confidence in him and the team as a whole.   He hoped that she knew how much of a big part she played in their success.   Without her he wasn’t sure they would have been able to get the entirety of the salvage back in one go.   Through the Force he let her know how thankful he was that she was here on the mission with him.   


When he had mentioned going back with the Wayfinder he felt the shift in her Force presence as well as her physical presence.   Confusion along with a new found need to understand what he was saying.   


He gave her hand a gentle squeeze as he gave her a small smile.  **”A lot Rin.  I’m still wrapping my head around it.   It’s a lot to unpack.”**  He took a deep breath to gather his thoughts.  He reached over to the controls and activated the cockpits recorder.    He didn’t want to have to try and recount this more than once in its entirety. 


**”The Wayfinder, is the *Key.*”**  He paused again.  **”There’s a lot to unpack so I apologize if I bounce around.   There is a Force path through the galaxy. Potentially even farther.  These Force paths are currents that aren’t charted.   They are only seen and used through the Force.  Separate from the known space lanes in the Galaxy.”**  He paused, taking a breath, as he let that sink in.

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
**”The Path, is broken.  The other Wayfinders are silent.  A Pathfinder is needed to reawaken the path and restore the connection.”**  He saw the confused look on Marina’s face and he decided he needed to shift slightly.  **”The Pathfinders are Force sensitive.  A secret sect of Force users charting the Path using the Force to navigate the hidden Force attuned hyperspace routes.”**  He nodded as he saw the realization dawning on her face.   **”Yes, it's a discovery that’s been lost for millennia.   Hidden in the Karthon Expanse.”**


He paused and looked to his left at the ship's controls.   All was still green and he had more to explain.  Looking back to Marina he continued.  **”What was relayed to me from the ship's Pathfinder was that something happened.  Some sort of chaotic Force energy.  *Whispers.*  These whispers corrupted the ship, its droids and crew.  The living crew simply shut down and died if I understood correctly.   The bridge crew, well their bodies, were still at their stations.”**  


**”Remember the lone body on the dias in main engineering?  That was the ship's Pathfinder.  He gave his life, pouring his life force into the ship, putting the ship into a deep dormancy.  Effectively shutting down the chaotic Force energy with some sort of paradox.   A state of deep dormancy.   The *Viel* would stay that way until someone came.  Someone who had the ability to release the dormancy.   You see the Pathfinder referred to the Wayfinder, as we call it, as the ‘Key’.   The Key isn’t just for guidance but it’s also for attunement.  The Key works with the Heart.”**  He knew he was getting deep into the explanation as Marina’s head tilted slightly to the side.   He smiled and chuckled softly.

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
**”I know this is a lot, bear with me for just a bit longer.”**  His eyes danced as he continued.   **”The Pathfinder used the Key to harmonize the Heart.  That will purge the *whispers* and calm the storm.”**  His voice lowered at the seriousness of his next words.  **”That without the Key the ship cannot be reawakened.  It would be madness to do so.  The storm would consume all.”**  


He gave her hand a squeeze.  He knew he had said a lot and it simply begged for more questions to be asked.  **"The last thing it said to me was that the Path was broken.  That the key needed to return.  Find the others and awaken…”** His voice trailed off and a sense of frustration came over him.    **”That was it.   The specter, Pathfinder, faded away.  Yet I feel that it still had more to tell.  Yet it had finally fulfilled its purpose.   To pass on what was needed.  To pass on the mission to another Pathfinder.  When I asked “why me” I wasn’t ready for its answer.  It said I was “Of the line and that the blood remembers the path.”  There was something about the spirit and the path.   I don’t know.  I hope that Chase and the Council will maybe have some idea or something is mentioned in the archives.”** 


He leaned back into his chair giving her hand a squeeze before letting go.   He reached over and turned off the recording.    **”There’s one more thing.  If I hadn’t done this, we would not have left and I would not have gotten the information that I did.”**  His dark eyes looked into hers.   There was a sense of something important coming.  **”I told the Pathfinder that I’d return with the Key.   I gave him my word that I would.”**

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
Marina sits in stunned silence, her hand held loosely in his, her mind reeling as Wes recounts the entirety of his encounter on the bridge. The exhaustion that had been pulling at her is completely forgotten, replaced by a wide-eyed, almost reverent awe. She listens to every word, her expression shifting from confusion to dawning comprehension, and finally to a profound understanding of the weight that has just been placed on his shoulders.

The pieces click into place with a terrifying, cosmic significance: the Wayfinder is a Key. The *Veiled Shadow* is a Lock. The Path is a lost network of Force-attuned hyperspace routes. The whispers, the droids, the sacrifice of the ancient Pathfinder... it's a story of a scale she can barely comprehend, a legacy that has been dormant for millennia, waiting.

And Wes... Wes is a descendant of this lost line of Force-navigators. The Pathfinder.

Her gaze is fixed on him, seeing him now not just as the Mandalorian warrior she fell in love with, or the Jedi Initiate finding his way, but as something more. Something ancient. The quiet confidence he's carried, his intuitive connection to the Force, the Wayfinder's immediate resonance with him—it all suddenly makes a chilling, profound kind of sense.

When he finishes, the cockpit is silent save for the hum of the hyperdrive. The weight of his final statement, his promise to the long-dead Pathfinder, hangs between them. She feels the immense burden of that promise, the sheer impossibility of it in their current circumstances, but she also feels his unwavering resolve.

She takes a slow, deep breath, her own mind racing to catch up. **"Wes..."** she says, her voice a hushed whisper. **"Of the line... a Pathfinder..."** She shakes her head slowly, not in disbelief, but in sheer awe. **"The Force... it truly does move in ways we can't predict."**

She squeezes his hand, her grip firm, a silent promise of her own. **"You gave your word. Then that's what we'll do."** There is no hesitation in her voice, no doubt. Her path has been intertwined with his from the beginning, and this new, impossible destiny is no different. **"When we get back to the *Chu'unthor*, we'll take this recording to Master Davion, to the Council. We'll make them understand. The Pathfinders... a way to travel outside the known galaxy, safe from the Empire... this is more than just salvage, Wes. This could be... everything. This could be the answer we've all been searching for."** Her eyes shine with a fierce, renewed hope, a purpose that transcends the simple need for survival and touches on the possibility of a true future.

As Marina spoke a small smile graced his face.   For he was so much in love with the woman who sat in front of him.   Without anything more said she was ready to jump in with both feet at the enormous task he had just explained.   He had no idea what was ahead of them but he knew he wouldn’t have to go it alone.  She knew what it meant for him to give his word.   That to go back on that went against all he had ever known.  Knowing that she would be at his side made the doubt fade away.  


When she finished he nodded as she was beginning to realize the potential implications of what he had told her.  Hidden hyperspace routes.    Ones that this new Empire didn’t know about.  To say that this was a massive discovery perhaps don’t even give it the importance it deserved.       


 **”Answers and potentially a whole lot more.  I can’t even begin to imagine what this could open up.  All I keep thinking is, why me?  Why us?”**  He gave her hand a squeeze as he looked at her.  His eyes looked lower at her growing belly and what that signified and a new realization came to him.   **”What has been uncovered doesn’t just have ties to me.   It has ties to us and now to our child who will have this same potential.”**  He took a deep breath as the galaxy seemed to be getting bigger and bigger by the second.

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
Marina's breath catches at his words. The implication, so obvious yet so profound, settles over her with the weight of a physical blow. Their child. A child of a Pathfinder, born to a Jedi, carrying a lineage that stretched back into the forgotten mists of galactic history, a lineage tied to this ancient, galaxy-spanning mystery.

Her hand, the one not holding his, comes to rest protectively on her own belly. The tiny, fluttering movements she'd felt earlier suddenly feel different, imbued with a new, almost terrifying significance. This wasn't just *their* child anymore; this was a continuation. An heir to a legacy she was only just beginning to comprehend.

**"Our child..."** she whispers, her eyes wide as she looks from her belly back to Wes. **"Of the line... Wes, if this is in your blood, then... then it's in theirs, too. They'll have this connection. This... this legacy."** The thought is overwhelming, a mixture of fierce, protective pride and a sudden, sharp fear for the burden their child might one day have to carry.

She tightens her grip on his hand, her mind racing. **"You're right. This isn't just about you anymore. It's about our family. About their future."** Her expression hardens with a new, unshakeable resolve. The mission, the Wayfinder, the Path—it's no longer just about survival or uncovering a mystery. It's about securing a heritage, a safe passage, a future for the life she carries. **"When we talk to the Council,"** she says, her voice now low and firm, all trace of fatigue gone, replaced by a mother's fierce determination, **"we're not just presenting a discovery. We're fighting for our child's birthright. A safe path. They *have* to understand."**

Wes saw and then felt her reaction to his words about their unborn child.  The implication of the possible future that was before them all hung heavy in the air.  He gave her hand a comforting squeeze.   **”This isn’t something we asked for Rin but this is something we will *fight* for.  The Council needs to understand.  Since my training began with the Jedi on the Chu’unthor I’ve heard that the Force does things for a reason.   Isn’t this a reason to find out why?”**  


He looked down at her belly.  Where their child was growing.  He gently placed his other hand over hers.  He could feel the little life that was growing inside her.  **”Lets talk to Master Davion first.  He had some knowledge of the Wayfinder.  He could help us talk to the Council.  Help make them understand the importance of this discovery.”**

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
Marina nods, her hand warm beneath his, her focus absolute. The layers of armor and suit fabric do little to dampen the profound connection between them, a current of shared resolve that flows stronger than any physical touch. **"You're right,"** she agrees, her voice steady. **"Master Davion... he understands. He saw your connection to the Wayfinder firsthand. He'll be our strongest advocate. We'll present the recording, explain everything we've learned, everything you've *felt*. We'll make our case to him first."**

She lets out a long, slow breath, the tension of the last few hours finally beginning to ease, replaced by a clear, if daunting, path forward. The steady hum of the hyperspace drive is a comforting, monotonous sound. She glances around the cramped but now-familiar cockpit of the *Seventh Moon*, a ship they'd literally brought back from the dead. **"But first,"** she says, a hint of her earlier weariness returning, mixed with a practical, almost wry tone, **"we have a few hours of quiet. And I would really, *really* like to get out of this suit."** She shifts slightly in the co-pilot's chair, the bulky EVA suit a cumbersome, restrictive shell. **"Do you think you could help me get out of this thing? I'm exhausted, and wrangling these seals is the last thing I have the energy for right now."** A tired but genuine smile touches her lips. **"I think I've had enough of playing spacer for one day."**

Wes nodded and smiled.  Her agreement in talking to Master Davion first bolstered his thinking.    He trusted her judgement.  She often looked at things differently.  He was initially trained as a hammer by his clan.  When one is trained to be a hammer all they saw was nails.  He’d gotten better with the ‘everything is a nail’ viewpoint  and it was good to have Marina’s insight.  **”Master Davion it is then.”**


With that settled he watched relax from the revelations they had just discussed.  He felt the shift in her presence.   Her mention of a few hours of quiet time and the need to shed the EVA suit had him smiling.  Watching her shift uncomfortably he nodded.  **”Yeah, let's get you out of that thing.   They weren’t really made for two were they.”** He said wryly as he stood offering the mother of his child and the woman he loved a hand up.

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
Marina lets out a soft, appreciative laugh, the sound a welcome note of levity after the intensity of their conversation. **"Definitely not made for two,"** she agrees, a tired but genuine smile gracing her lips. She takes his offered hand, the grip of his armored glove firm and steady as he helps her rise from the co-pilot's chair. The simple act of standing is more of an effort in the bulky suit than she'd care to admit.

**"The cargo hold would be better,"** she says, gesturing towards the cockpit door. **"More room to maneuver. Besides, we'll need to stow the suit there anyway."**

They make their way back through the short corridor to the Ghtroc's main hold. The two colossal power converters, now securely clamped to the deck, dominate the space, silent and immense. Roric is nowhere to be seen, likely having found one of the spartan crew bunks for some much-needed rest.

The hold, though filled with their salvage, offers them the space they need. Wes, with a practiced efficiency born from years of dealing with his own armor, helps Marina with the heavy, awkward seals at the back of her EVA suit. He works with a gentle, patient care, disengaging the magnetic locks at her neck and shoulders. Finally, with a last hiss of depressurizing seals, the main torso of the suit comes free.

Marina steps out of it with a deep sigh of relief, clad now in just her simple, practical Jedi under-tunic and trousers. The absence of the suit's bulk makes the gentle, distinct curve of her twenty-week pregnancy more apparent. She stretches, her hands going to the small of her back, a weary but contented expression on her face. **"Much better,"** she murmurs. **"Thank you, Wes."** She leans against him for a moment, resting her head against the cool, solid plate of his beskar chest armor, soaking in the quiet comfort of his presence.

**”Here, turn around.”** Wes said as he made a circular motion with his finger.  **”I’ll have you free of this in no time.”**  Even though she could see his face she heard the smirk in his voice as the maglock was disengaged at her neck.   **”I don’t think they had pregnant women in mind for these suits.”**  He said jokingly as he loosened the EVA suit.


It didn’t take long until he was watching her stretch out the kinks in her limbs.    He smiled as he saw the telltale curve of her abdomen.   Looking back up to her face his smile remained.  Despite the grueling mission and the time spent in the EVA suit she was glowing.   


When she held him she sensed that he wished the armor wasn’t in the way.  It had been a long mission and he wished they could find some time to rest.   Looking down he saw that her head rested on a particularly ugly scar on his chest plate.  The electro staff of the Custodian had left its mark.  While their mission went better than anyone had expected it wasn’t without its dangers.  A toll had been taken on them all.  

He rubbed her back gently as he bent his head down to kiss the top of her head gently.  **”Do you want to take the few hours we have to get some rest?  I’m sure when we get to the Chu’unthor we will again be busy for a while.”**

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
**"No, I don't think 'maternity EVA' was high on their design priority list,"** Marina agrees, her voice laced with weary amusement as she rolls her shoulders, working out the stiffness. She gratefully leans into his embrace, her head coming to rest against his chest plate, right over the fresh battle scar. She feels the hard, damaged edge of the beskar through her tunic and her fingers come up to gently trace its outline, a silent acknowledgment of the danger he'd faced, of the price of their success. She feels his kiss on the top of her head and lets out a soft, contented sigh.

The idea of rest is incredibly appealing. She's bone-tired, the adrenaline of the mission and the power-up sequence finally giving way to a profound exhaustion. But as she looks around the dusty, silent hold of the *Seventh Moon*, her practical nature takes over. **"Rest sounds like heaven, Wes,"** she murmurs, her voice muffled against his armor. **"But... not in here."**

She pulls back just enough to look around the hold, a slight wrinkle in her nose. **"This place hasn't been cleaned in seventy-five years. The air recyclers are working, but everything is coated in a layer of dust and grime."** She glances towards the small, dark corridor that leads to the crew quarters. **"The bunks... I can't imagine what state they're in. We'd be breathing in a century of dust."**

She looks back up at him, her expression a mixture of practicality and a longing for comfort. **"The cockpit is the cleanest, most sealed part of the ship. Maybe... if we can wipe down the chairs... we could rest there? It's not a real bed, but at least we won't be sleeping on who knows what."**

Wes smiled at her comment about not wanting to rest here in the cargo hold.  **”Agreed not in here.  This ship needs an entire going over and not just mechanically.”**  He said as he looked around.  The fine dusting of partials that covered the *Viel* had also made its way on the the *Moon*.    **”Im sure we can find something to wipe down the cockpit to make it a little better.  I’m sure Roric has already found a place to get comfortable.  Might as well do the same.  Besides with how old this ship is I’d rather be in the cockpit in case something decides to happen.”**  


He stepped back slightly from the embrace and held her hand.  **”Back to the cockpit and let’s see if we can get some rest before we arrive at the Chu’unthor.”**


Together they walked back to the cockpit hand in hand relishing the small amount of downtime they had.   It wasn’t much but after the mission Wes was willing to take what he could get.    


Managing to find cleaning items, and what was now old hand towels.  Wes had managed to at least dust off and wipe down the cockpits chairs and consuls.   It wasn't a deep clean but it took off the main layer of fine space dust that had premiated everywhere.   


He watched as Marina did what she could to make herself comfortable.  It wasn’t ideal but it beat staying in the EVA suit and now she was at least able to sit comfortably.   He sat next to her in the pilot's chair and looked over the console.   Everything was still looking good and they still had a few hours to rest.   Looking over at Marina he saw that she was already settled in.   Her calm Force presence told him she was already sleeping.  


He made himself comfortable.

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content:
The next few hours pass in the quiet, humming solitude of hyperspace. Wes and Marina rest as best they can in the ancient cockpit chairs of the *Seventh Moon*, the steady thrum of the engines a surprisingly comforting lullaby. The Ghtroc, a ghost brought back to life, proves its reliability, holding true to the course Marina had plotted.

A soft chime from the navicomputer rouses Wes from a light, fitful doze. The streaking blue-white lines of hyperspace outside the viewport resolve back into the familiar, star-dusted blackness of realspace. They have arrived back at the edge of the Karthon system.

Almost immediately, a tight-beam comm request comes in. It's Kael. **"***Seventh Moon*, *Void Skimmer*. Good to see your signal. We're holding at the pre-jump rally point. Transmitting final approach data for Karthos Prime now. It's a short, fifteen-minute micro-jump to clear the outer debris field, then sublight to the *Chu'unthor's* orbit. Acknowledge."**

Marina, stirring from her own rest, quickly brings her console to life. **"Solid copy, *Skimmer*,"** she replies, her voice clear and alert. **"Receiving final approach data. We'll slave our nav-computer to your jump timing. See you on the other side."**

The data flows in, and Marina inputs the final commands. The *Seventh Moon* aligns itself with the distant *Void Skimmer*, and on an unseen countdown, both ships lurch forward, the stars elongating once more for the final, brief jump.

Fifteen minutes later, they emerge into a scene that is both chaotic and beautiful. They are in the upper atmosphere of Karthos Prime, a swirling maelstrom of planetary rings and orbital debris, all of it bathed in the soft, emerald glow of the jungle world below. And in the center of it all, holding a steady, defiant orbit, is the massive, familiar form of the *Chu'unthor II*, its running lights a welcome beacon in the dark. Their home.

As the stars elongated once again Wes opened the *Moon’s* internal comms.  **”We’re out of the Karthon Expanse Roric.   In fifteen minutes we’ll be jumping out near the Chu’unthor.   We’re almost home.”**  


He glanced over the controls once more before looking over at Marina.   He saw her doing the same.  Clear blue eyes looking over the ship's internal and external sensors.  If anything was going to go amiss she’d spot it.  


He thought about the upcoming talk with Master Chase and then the Council.   Going back to the *Veiled Shadow* wasn’t going to be an easy sell to the council.   His feelings must have been strong as he saw Marina look over at him.   He felt a sense of warmth and conviction from her.   He knew that she believed in him.  He just hoped that the Council would too.   


————


Together the three of them watched the timer count down.   As it hit zero the stars stretched once again as they reverted back to real space.   Before them was a welcome sight.  Admits the swirling maelstrom of colors was the *Chu’unthor II*  There she waited for them to come home.   


Reaching over he flipped the comms switch.   **”Chu’unthor II,* this is Del-Fin piloting the *Seventh Moon.* We’re home.   Transmitting security codes.  Have maintenance teams meet us in the hangar bay.”**  He smiled as he spoke.   **”We’ll see you when we land.  Del-Fin out.”** Hitting the transmit butting he sent the codes off to the *Chu’unthor.*  


**”Almost home.”**

author.username: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3
author.global_name: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

userName: Wes Del-Fin ●  HP▕▆ 76/92; CT 0 • FP 7; DP 3

content: A moment after Wes sends the security codes, a familiar, authoritative voice responds, cutting cleanly through the comms. It's Commander Lyra Heston from the *Chu'unthor's* bridge.

***"Solid copy, *Seventh Moon*. Your codes are green. Welcome home, Del-Fin,"*** she says, her tone crisp and professional, but perhaps with a faint, almost imperceptible hint of relief. ***"Hangar Bay A-42 is being prepped for your arrival. We are also receiving a transmission from the *Void Skimmer*, confirming their successful return. Medical and engineering teams will be on standby. Proceed to docking vector seven-niner. Heston out."***

Marina lets out a long, slow breath she didn't realize she was holding, a wide, tired smile spreading across her face. **"Hangar A-42... they're giving us the main bay,"** she says, the significance of the gesture not lost on her. **"They must be serious about this salvage."** She looks over at Wes, her eyes shining with a mixture of pride, relief, and utter exhaustion. **"Almost home,"** she echoes, her voice soft.

Wes guides the old Ghtroc freighter with a steady hand, following the designated approach vector. The massive form of the *Chu'unthor II* grows larger and larger in the viewport, its docking bay lights a warm, welcoming beacon in the cold of space. The chaotic energy of the mission begins to recede, replaced by the steady, familiar presence of home.

The *Seventh Moon* glides smoothly into the immense, brightly lit expanse of Hangar Bay A-42. The magnetic docking clamps engage with a series of heavy, reassuring **THUNKS**, and the ship settles onto the deck with a final, gentle hiss of its repulsorlifts. Kael, having landed the *Void Skimmer* in an adjacent berth moments earlier, is already on the deck, directing a team of ground crew.

As the *Moon's* engines whine down, a profound silence falls over the cockpit, broken only by the soft, blinking lights of the console.

Marina lets out a long, shuddering sigh, the tension of the last few days finally releasing its grip. She leans back in her co-pilot's chair, her eyes closing for a brief moment as the low hum of the now-docked *Seventh Moon* surrounds them. They're safe. They're home.

She opens her eyes again, her gaze falling on the complex series of shutdown sequences on her console. Her mechanic's mind, ever-dutiful, kicks back into gear despite the overwhelming exhaustion.

**"Alright,"** she says, her voice soft but steady. **"I need to run the post-flight diagnostics and begin the cold shutdown procedure for the core. We can't just leave it idling after a cold start like that."** She glances over at Wes, a tired but affectionate smile on her face. **"You go,"** she urges gently. **"Go deal with the deck crews, coordinate with Kael, give your preliminary report to Commander Heston. I've got this. I'll make sure she's put to bed properly."**

With the go ahead from Commander Heston Wes keyed the comms.   **”Copy Bridge.  Hangar Bay A-42.   Del-Fin out.”**  With the go ahead confirmed he found himself taking a deep breath and letting it out as Marina did the same.   Her comment about Hangar A-42 had him nodding as well.  **”Maybe the situation has worsened.  We’ll know more when we land.  Either way  the sooner they get this cargo to where it needed to be the better.”**


It was not more than another fifteen minutes until they heard the loud ***THUNKS*** of the docking clamps engaging with the *Moon.*  With practiced hands and an increasing familiarity with the Ghtroc’s controls Wes  shut down the repulsors and pulled back the power to the main engines to minimal output.  


Letting out a thankful sigh at the stillness of the cockpit he turned his chair towards Marina.   He wasn’t surprised to see her already beginning shut down procedures on the *Moon*.  Her smile and explanation was expected.  Reaching for and grabbing his helmet he stood.  Taking a step closer he bent down and gave her a quick kiss.   He knew that dragging her out of here was going to be impossible until she did what she felt was needed.  **”Alright Cyar’ika.   I’ll send a couple techs up to give you a hand.  I’m not sure how long this will take or when they will want the debrief.  One thing is for sure we will talk with Master Davion ASAP.”**  He watched as her hands moved fluidly over the controls.   He gave her a pat on the shoulder letting the electrical sensation of their touching pass between them.  


————


With a tap of the gang plank controls the rap began to lower.  Wes looked over at Roric as he tucked his helmet beneath his left arm.  He gave the veteran soldier a smile.  **”Good work out there, Roric.  I’d be happy to work again with any of you, anytime.”** He patted the man on the back of the shoulder as he started to descend the gangplank.

Seeing a couple of technicians heading his way he waved them over.  As they approached Wes saw that they were a senior and junior tech.  **”Can you two go aboard and help Padawan Cloudspinner in the cockpit?  She’s going to shut down the *Moon.*  Give her a hand and see that she gets what she needs.”**  Getting a nod from the senior tech Wes smiled and nodded.   **”Thank you both.”**  


As he moved around to the starboard side of the *Moon.* he could hear Kael talking to some ground crew giving them directions.   Together he and Roric advanced on the group.  **”Good to see you Kael.”**

Roric follows Wes down the ramp, his own helmet now tucked under his arm. He gives Wes a rare, almost-smile. **"Likewise, Del-Fin,"** he grunts, the highest form of praise the stoic soldier is likely to give. **"You lead a clean op. Anytime."**

The two technicians Wes flagged down nod respectfully and immediately head up the ramp into the *Seventh Moon* to assist Marina. As Wes and Roric approach the small group gathered near the *Void Skimmer*, Kael turns from directing a cargo-lift team.

Kael turns from the ground crew, his usual impassive expression breaking into a rare, faint smirk as he sees Wes and Roric approach. **"Del-Fin. Jannsen,"** he greets them with a curt nod. **"So, you decided to bring the whole scrapheap back with you. Ambitious."** He glances at the fresh, ugly scar on the *Seventh Moon's* mandible. **"Looks like she put up a bit of a fight on the way out."**

Before Kael can offer any more dry commentary, another group approaches with a determined stride. Commander Lyra Heston leads the way, her expression as stern and focused as ever, flanked by the lumbering, grease-stained form of Chief Engineer Rorin Gaxx, who is practically vibrating with a mixture of exhaustion and intense, barely contained excitement.

**"Initiate Del-Fin,"** Heston begins without preamble, her eyes sweeping over Wes, Roric, and the newly arrived *Seventh Moon*. **"A preliminary report, now. Confirm your primary and secondary objectives were secured."**

**"And the converters, Del-Fin, the converters!"** Gaxx interrupts, his large Mon Calamari eyes wide. **"My techs are saying you brought back two High-Yield units. Are they viable? Did you get a look at the core matrices? Are they the Omega series or something else?"**
Heston silences the Chief Engineer with a sharp glance. **"The details can wait for the full debrief, Chief. Initiate Del-Fin, your report."**

Wes looked up at the damaged mandible on the *Moon.*  He shook his head, the bone jarring scraping and tearing of the ship's hull a not so distant reminder.  **”Yeah I wasn’t expecting the gravitational force…”**  He stopped the explanation as Commander Heston, who was flanked by Chief Engineer Gaxx, quickly approached.   She looked focused and Wes found himself wondering if she ever smiled.   


When she addressed him he nodded. He was about to answer but Chief Gaxx was anxious and cut him off.  Wes could feel the irritation flash from the commander.     **”Commander, all objectives were completed.  The cargo is split between both transports.  If we hadn’t come across the *Moon* here we would not have gotten it all back.  Well in one trip anyway.”**  He looked at Chief Gaxx.  **”Padawan Cloudspinner can confirm the specifics on the converters but they should be what you’re looking for, Chief.”**  He gave the Mon Cal a nod and a smile.  **”Padawan Cloudspinner is aboard the *Moon* along with the converters.  She’s doing a full shutdown on the *Moon’s* engines right now.”**


He looked back at Commander Heston.  **”How have things been since we left?”**

Commander Heston gives a single, sharp nod, absorbing Wes's concise report. **"All objectives secured and an additional asset recovered. A successful operation, Initiate. Your leadership is noted."** Her tone is all business, but the acknowledgment is clear praise from the stoic XO.

In response to his question, her expression remains grim. **"The situation remains... critical, Del-Fin. Team Alpha's shuttle returned an hour ago with a haul of unrefined fuel ore, but Team Beta encountered heavy resistance at the medical station and were forced to withdraw with only minimal supplies. We sustained two casualties, thankfully not critical. Your success here is a significant relief, but we are still operating on a razor's edge."**

She lets the grim news hang in the air for a moment. **"Your team's success is, at present, the only unqualified positive outcome of Operation: Karthon Gambit. The resources you've brought back are now more critical than ever."** She consults her datapad presumably to check both the time (currently 0130) and the schedule. **"You and your team are relieved. Report to the main briefing room at 1400 for a full debriefing with the Council and department heads. Get some rest. You've earned it."** With a final, decisive nod, she turns and strides away, likely to oversee the critical process of offloading the salvage.

Chief Gaxx, having heard that the converters are aboard, looks torn between interrogating Wes further and rushing onto the *Seventh Moon* himself. He gives a series of excited, gurgling clicks. **"Viable! She says they're viable!"** he mutters to himself, then looks at Wes, his large eyes wide with a manic, engineering glee. **"Good work, son, good work! Don't you worry, we'll take care of your ship. We'll take good care of her!"** With that, he and a team of his most senior technicians practically sprint towards the *Seventh Moon's* ramp, tools already in hand, eager to inspect their prize.

Wes listened as Commander Heston gave him a quick rundown of the other two teams and the outcome of their two missions.   One sounded like a success and the other only marginally so.   He gave the commander a small nod as she continued on about his team's success.  It was then he remembered words from his father.  A leader is only as good as the people he leads.   He had a very good team that made him look good.   **”I had a very good team Commander.  I’d work with any of them again.”**   


When she relieved him and the team he nodded.  **”Understood.  I’ll let my team know to report at 1400 hours.”**   With a final nod she was turning and off to the next crisis.   It was then he heard Chief Gaxx talking with the glee of a child at hearing that the converters were viable.   Wes’s own smile grew at the Mon Cal’s growing enthusiasm for the cargo they brought back.   He watched as Gaxx turned and moved quickly got the *Moon’s* Gangplank.   


He brought his bracer computer up and recorded a message.  **”Full debriefing at 1400 hours in the main briefing room.   See you all then.  Get some rest.   You have all earned it.”**  He listened to the message and then sent it to the team.   


Looking at the ship that brought them home he reached out and felt Marina's presence back in the engineering section.   With a quick breath he made his way to engineering passing a couple of Gaxx’s techs who were excitedly talking about the converters.     Passing the threshold to where Marina was he stepped closer as she was looking over the consoles that were relaying the ship’s diagnostic on a couple screens.   **”Padawan Cloudspinner we’ve been ordered to get some rest.”**  He said just loud enough for her to hear.

Marina looks up from the engineering console, her expression a mixture of deep focus and profound weariness. The chaotic energy of the mission has faded, leaving a bone-deep exhaustion in its wake. She offers Wes a small, tired smile as he enters.

**"Ordered, huh?"** she replies, her voice soft. **"That's one order I won't have any trouble following."** She taps a final command into the console, and the low hum of the auxiliary systems fades into a deeper quiet. **"Alright, she's in a stable cold-shutdown state. Gaxx's team can take it from here."**

She pushes herself away from the console, her movements slow and deliberate. She reaches out, her hand finding his armored gauntlet, her grip a silent expression of relief and shared victory. **"Let's go home, Wes,"** she says, her blue eyes holding his. **"Let's go get some rest."**

Hand in hand, they walk from the engine room of the silent *Seventh Moon*, leaving the newly acquired ship to the excited ministrations of Chief Gaxx and his engineering teams. They cross the bustling hangar bay, a quiet island of shared exhaustion and triumph amidst the organized chaos of the returning expedition teams. The journey back to their own small cabin on Deck 15 is a slow, weary trek, each step a testament to the trials they have endured and overcome.

Inside their quarters, the silence is a welcome balm. There are no more missions to plan, no more ancient droids to fight, no more desperate gambles to take—at least for tonight. The Wayfinder and its profound, galaxy-spanning mysteries can wait. The dire state of the fleet and the coming debrief can wait. For now, there is only the quiet comfort of their shared space, a hard-won sanctuary in a hostile galaxy.

The mission is over. They have returned, not just with critical salvage, but with a new ship, a new, daunting purpose, and a bond forged and tested in the silent, ancient darkness between the stars. As the door to their quarters slides shut, sealing them away from the wounded, struggling Praxeum ship, they have found, if only for a few precious hours, a moment of peace.
